title: The quiet power of deep work
author: Leah Morgan
dek: In a world full of pings, tabs and interruptions, focus has quietly become a superpower.
body: There is a particular kind of tiredness that comes from being busy all day and still feeling you have made no meaningful progress. It is not a lack of effort; often, it is the result of attention being pulled in too many directions.

Deep work is the opposite: a stretch of time spent on one cognitively demanding task without distraction. Its value lies not only in finishing faster, but in noticing connections that scattered attention would miss.
Focus is not a personality trait reserved for a lucky few. It is an environment we can learn to design by protecting the moments that deserve our best attention.
question: What is the writer's main point?
options: Deep work means working longer hours.
Focus is a skill we can actively cultivate.
Technology is always harmful to productivity.
explanation: The conclusion presents focus as something that can be deliberately designed and practised.

title: When an algorithm remembers for us
author: Amir Patel
dek: Digital memory is convenient, but convenience can subtly change what we choose to remember.
body: Our devices now archive photographs, locations and conversations with a precision no personal diary could match. Yet an abundant record is not the same as a meaningful memory; the former stores everything, while the latter selects.

When an app resurfaces an old image, it supplies a prompt but also a frame. We may remember the event through the software's chronology rather than through the emotions that originally gave it weight.

The challenge is not to reject digital archives, but to remain authors of our own recollections. A useful archive should support reflection, not quietly decide which version of the past deserves our attention.
question: What concern does the writer raise about digital archives?
options: They are too expensive for most people.
They can influence how people interpret their own past.
They prevent people from taking photographs.
explanation: The reading argues that software can frame memories, not merely store them.

title: The second life of broken things
author: Maya Chen
dek: Repair cafes are changing the status of objects from disposable purchases to shared responsibilities.
body: At a repair cafe, a toaster with a temperamental switch sits beside a jacket missing a zip and a laptop that refuses to wake. Visitors bring objects, but they also bring questions, patience and a willingness to learn from strangers.

The practical result is modest: fewer items enter the waste stream. The social result is more ambitious. Repair creates a setting in which expertise circulates horizontally, allowing a retired engineer and a curious teenager to contribute to the same solution.
These spaces challenge the assumption that convenience is the highest form of progress. Sometimes progress is simply extending an object's useful life while strengthening the relationships around it.
question: Why does the writer regard repair cafes as socially important?
options: They sell cheaper replacement products.
They allow practical knowledge to circulate between people.
They are mainly places for retired engineers.
explanation: The reading stresses shared learning and cooperation alongside waste reduction.

title: How languages change without permission
author: Nora Ellis
dek: Language change is less a decline from a golden age than a record of people adapting to new lives.
body: Every generation complains that the next one is damaging the language. However, the verbs, pronunciations and expressions once treated as errors often reveal how communities have moved, mixed and made new tools their own.

A word can travel from a specialist field into ordinary speech; a borrowed phrase can become so familiar that its foreign origin disappears. These shifts are not random, although they may be difficult to notice while they are happening.
To study language change is therefore to study social change. The most revealing question is not whether a new expression is pure, but what need made speakers reach for it.
question: What attitude toward language change does the writer adopt?
options: It is mainly evidence of cultural decline.
It is a record of communities adapting to change.
It happens randomly and cannot be studied.
explanation: The writer rejects the idea of simple decline and connects linguistic shifts to social needs.

title: The architecture of a pause
author: Jon Bell
dek: Good public spaces do not merely move people through a city; they give them reasons to stop.
body: A bench under a tree may seem like an afterthought compared with a dramatic station or a polished plaza. Yet small invitations to pause can determine whether a street feels like a route to endure or a place to inhabit.

Designers increasingly study the unplanned behaviours that occur between destinations: a conversation in shade, a child watching a delivery bicycle, a passer-by lingering near a fountain. Such moments are difficult to measure, but they are central to belonging.
The best urban design is therefore not always the most visible. It makes room for unscheduled life and treats rest as a civic function rather than an interruption to movement.
question: What does the writer suggest is a key function of public design?
options: To make every building visually dramatic.
To encourage people to inhabit places, not just pass through them.
To eliminate all unplanned behaviour.
explanation: The reading contrasts movement with lingering and presents pause as part of civic life.

title: Can a label change a meal?
author: Priya Shah
dek: Carbon labels may turn an invisible environmental cost into a factor people can weigh before they buy.
body: Most shoppers can compare the price and weight of two meals immediately. Their climate impact is less legible, hidden in farming practices, transport, refrigeration and packaging that occurred long before the product reached the shelf.

A carbon label makes that chain visible, but visibility alone does not guarantee a different choice. People also need affordable alternatives, trustworthy measurements and a sense that individual action is connected to wider change.

The most promising labels are therefore not moral scorecards. They are invitations to compare, helping consumers make informed decisions while keeping responsibility shared by producers, retailers and policymakers.
question: What limitation of carbon labels does the writer acknowledge?
options: They cannot be printed on food packaging.
Information alone may not change behaviour without accessible alternatives.
They only measure transport emissions.
explanation: The writer says labels need to be accompanied by affordability, reliable data and systemic responsibility.

title: The residue of divided attention
author: Ruth Okafor
dek: Switching tasks may feel efficient, yet part of the mind can remain attached to the previous problem.
body: A notification interrupts a report, a message interrupts the notification and soon a worker is moving rapidly without fully arriving anywhere. The visible cost is lost time; the less visible cost is the mental residue left by unfinished tasks.

This residue helps explain why a short interruption can make a complex problem feel disproportionately difficult. Attention does not instantly return to its previous depth, especially when the interrupted task was demanding or emotionally charged.

The practical lesson is not to ban every interruption. It is to group shallow tasks, protect recovery time and recognise that switching has a cognitive price even when the calendar makes it look free.
question: What practical lesson does the writer draw?
options: All workplace messages should be banned.
Task switching has a cognitive cost that should be planned for.
Complex problems should be replaced with simpler tasks.
explanation: The final paragraph recommends planning around, rather than pretending to eliminate, the cost of switching.

title: The amateurs who map a changing planet
author: Sofia Reed
dek: Citizen science works best when enthusiasm is paired with careful methods and meaningful feedback.
body: A birdwatcher with a notebook, a diver with an underwater camera and a gardener recording flowering dates may appear far removed from a research laboratory. Together, however, their observations can reveal patterns too widespread for a small professional team to capture.

The strength of citizen science is scale, but scale introduces noise. Projects must teach participants what to record, explain how uncertain observations will be handled and show contributors what their data helped uncover.

Participation becomes durable when volunteers are treated as partners rather than unpaid sensors. The exchange is then both scientific and civic: people learn how evidence is made while helping describe a changing world.
question: According to the writer, what makes citizen science sustainable?
options: Replacing professional researchers entirely.
Combining broad participation with clear methods and feedback.
Asking volunteers to collect as much data as possible without instruction.
explanation: The writer identifies scale as useful only when supported by method, transparency and reciprocity.

title: The museum behind the museum
author: Clara Voss
dek: What visitors see is only a fraction of the work required to keep a collection alive.
body: Galleries suggest stillness: objects rest under controlled lights while visitors move quietly between them. Behind the walls, conservators monitor humidity, document tiny changes and make decisions about which marks are damage and which are part of an object's history.

Storage is not a passive warehouse. It is a system of priorities shaped by space, funding and interpretation. An item kept out of sight may be protected, forgotten or awaiting a future question that the museum has not yet learned to ask.
To understand a museum fully, we must therefore look beyond exhibition design. The unseen labour of care determines which stories remain available for public attention.
question: What does the writer imply about museum storage?
options: It is a neutral space with no interpretive consequences.
It involves choices that affect which stories remain visible.
It is less important than the lighting in galleries.
explanation: The reading presents storage as an active system of priorities, not a neutral warehouse.

title: Shade as public infrastructure
author: Elena Ruiz
dek: As cities heat up, shade is becoming an essential service rather than a decorative extra.
body: A tree-lined avenue and a concrete avenue may have identical widths, yet they offer radically different experiences on a summer afternoon. Shade lowers surface temperatures, makes walking possible and gives older residents a reason to remain outdoors.

Planting trees is not a quick fix. Roots need space, species must suit the climate and maintenance must be funded for decades. Still, the choice to provide shade distributes comfort and safety more fairly than relying on private air-conditioned interiors.
Seen this way, urban canopy is not ornamental. It is a form of infrastructure whose benefits are measured in access, health and the freedom to move through the city.
question: Why does the writer call shade infrastructure?
options: It makes streets look more expensive.
It provides practical benefits that support public access and health.
It removes the need for any maintenance.
explanation: Shade is described as enabling movement, comfort and health across public space.

title: The case for slower news
author: Daniel Cho
dek: When every update competes to arrive first, accuracy and context can become casualties.
body: Breaking news is built around the urgency of the present, but many stories become more useful after the first wave of certainty has passed. A slower report can distinguish what is known from what is merely circulating, and can explain why an event matters beyond the headline.
This does not mean abandoning immediacy. It means recognising that speed is one journalistic value among several, not the measure by which every form of reporting should be judged.

Readers may discover that waiting for a considered account feels less satisfying than refreshing a feed. Yet the reward is a public record less vulnerable to rumour, correction and collective overreaction.
question: What does the writer argue should accompany speed in journalism?
options: More dramatic headlines.
Accuracy and context.
A complete refusal to report breaking events.
explanation: The text explicitly frames speed as one value that must be balanced with accuracy and context.

title: Recipes as family archives
author: Mina Laurent
dek: A handwritten recipe records more than ingredients; it preserves habits, substitutions and the memory of a household.
body: A recipe card often leaves out the information a stranger would need: how soft the butter should be, which pan counts as small or when the cook's judgement says the sauce is ready. These omissions are not mistakes so much as evidence that the card belonged to a living practice.

As recipes travel between generations, they accumulate annotations. A missing ingredient becomes a local substitute; a long instruction is shortened because its meaning is now shared. The document changes while its emotional authority grows.
Reading such cards as archives reveals history at domestic scale. Migration, scarcity and affection appear not as abstract forces, but as choices made around an ordinary table.
question: Why are omissions in family recipes significant?
options: They prove the original cook lacked skill.
They show the recipe was part of a shared living practice.
They make the recipes impossible to pass on.
explanation: The missing details assume shared knowledge, showing that the card belonged to an ongoing household practice.

title: What a sabbatical is really for
author: Marcus Green
dek: A meaningful career break is less about escape than about creating distance from automatic decisions.
body: People often describe a sabbatical as time away from work, but absence alone does not guarantee renewal. Without a change in rhythm, the same habits of urgency can simply migrate into a different setting.

The most useful breaks create room for low-stakes experiments: studying a question, volunteering in an unfamiliar field or returning to a neglected craft. These activities generate evidence about what a person values before a new commitment is made.

A sabbatical therefore works as a form of deliberate uncertainty. It does not provide a perfect answer about the future; it interrupts the momentum that might otherwise make the answer feel inevitable.
question: What is the main purpose of a meaningful sabbatical?
options: To avoid making any future commitment.
To create distance that allows new evidence about one's priorities.
To reproduce the same work habits in another country.
explanation: The writer values distance and low-stakes experiments because they challenge automatic career momentum.

title: The invisible map of urban noise
author: Ibrahim Wells
dek: Noise maps reveal that an unequal soundscape can shape health and opportunity as much as a visible pollutant.
body: Traffic, construction and nightlife do not distribute themselves evenly. A map of decibel levels often follows older patterns of housing inequality, placing the loudest corridors beside communities with the fewest resources to escape them.

Because noise is difficult to photograph, it can be dismissed as an annoyance rather than treated as an environmental exposure. Yet persistent sound affects sleep, concentration and the ability to recover from stress.

Mapping makes the problem politically legible. It cannot solve the imbalance by itself, but it gives residents a shared language for asking who is asked to tolerate disturbance and who is allowed quiet.
question: What does mapping noise make possible?
options: It makes all city sounds disappear.
It turns a hard-to-see inequality into evidence for public debate.
It proves that traffic is the only source of noise.
explanation: The writer says mapping makes the unequal soundscape visible and politically discussable.

title: Letting a river find its shape
author: Tara Singh
dek: River restoration increasingly means removing control rather than adding another layer of concrete.
body: For decades, many rivers were straightened, narrowed and separated from their floodplains in the name of efficiency. The result was a channel that moved water quickly but left surrounding habitats fragile and downstream communities exposed when exceptional rainfall arrived.
Rewilding projects take a different approach. By reconnecting wetlands and allowing bends to return, they slow water, create habitat and give floods somewhere to spread before they become disasters.
The work can look untidy compared with a manicured embankment. Its success is measured less by visual order than by resilience: a river is healthier when it has room to behave like a river.
question: Why might a restored river appear untidy?
options: Because restoration removes all vegetation.
Because natural processes are allowed to replace rigid visual control.
Because flooding is considered desirable in every situation.
explanation: The reading contrasts manicured embankments with a more flexible, natural river system.

title: Buildings that refuse to retire
author: Theo Martin
dek: Adaptive reuse asks whether an old building's next life can be more valuable than its demolition.
body: A warehouse designed for storage may become a library; a former school may become housing. Adaptive reuse preserves the physical memory of a place while allowing its function to change, often with less material waste than rebuilding from scratch.

The approach is not automatically sustainable. Old structures can be inefficient, and preserving them may require compromises in accessibility or energy use. Its value lies in making those trade-offs visible rather than pretending that a clean slate has no cost.
A reused building becomes a negotiation between inherited form and present need. The result may be imperfect, but it can connect a community's future to a past that would otherwise be erased.
question: What trade-off does the writer say adaptive reuse can involve?
options: It always costs more than demolition.
Preserving an old structure may conflict with efficiency or accessibility.
It prevents buildings from changing function.
explanation: The writer acknowledges that reuse can require compromises, even while avoiding the material cost of demolition.

title: Who owns a digital afterlife?
author: Nadia Brooks
dek: The data left behind by a person continues to circulate after their ability to consent has ended.
body: A dead person's account may contain private messages, photographs and a public identity that relatives do not fully understand. Platforms often offer memorial settings, but these are technical solutions to a question that is also emotional and legal: who has the authority to shape the remaining record?
Families may disagree about whether access is a form of care or a violation. The person who died may also have left instructions that are incomplete, contradictory or impossible to locate.

Digital inheritance therefore exposes a gap between old ideas of property and new forms of presence. The hardest problem is not storage; it is deciding how respect should work when a life remains visible but can no longer speak for itself.
question: What problem does digital inheritance expose?
options: That online accounts are impossible to store.
That older ideas of property do not easily cover continuing digital presence.
That families always agree about memorial accounts.
explanation: The conclusion identifies a mismatch between traditional property concepts and persistent digital identities.

title: The things translation cannot carry
author: Yuki Sato
dek: Translation is not a transparent window; it is a series of choices about rhythm, context and what a reader should feel.
body: A translator can preserve the literal meaning of a sentence and still lose its social temperature. A joke may become an explanation, a formal greeting may sound intimate, and a rhythm built for one language may arrive in another wearing an unfamiliar shape.

There is no single solution because translation serves different purposes. A legal document values precision, while a novel may require the translator to recreate ambiguity or music rather than reproduce each word.

The best translations do not pretend that nothing changed. They make a new path between languages, acknowledging the distance while allowing a reader to experience something more important than a dictionary equivalent: intention.
question: What does the writer suggest a good translation should preserve?
options: Every word in exactly the same order.
The intended effect, even when the form must change.
Only the literal meaning of technical terms.
explanation: The reading presents translation as recreating intention and effect, not copying surface form mechanically.

title: The hidden tax of too many choices
author: Helen Park
dek: Decision fatigue is not a failure of willpower; it is often a design problem created by repeated low-value choices.
body: A person who spends the morning choosing among notifications, subscription plans and minor household tasks may arrive at an important decision already depleted. The individual choices are small, but their accumulation consumes the attention needed for judgement.
Routines can reduce this tax, yet routines are sometimes dismissed as boring or inflexible. Their quiet benefit is that they reserve deliberate thought for decisions that genuinely deserve it.
The point is not to eliminate choice from life. It is to distinguish between choices that express a value and choices that merely repeat a decision the environment could have made once.
question: How does the writer propose reducing decision fatigue?
options: By refusing to make important decisions.
By using routines for repetitive, low-value choices.
By making every part of life spontaneous.
explanation: The writer recommends reserving conscious effort for meaningful decisions and automating repetition through routines.

title: The first neighbourhood on the Moon
author: Owen Price
dek: A lunar settlement would test not only engineering, but the social assumptions built into the idea of exploration.
body: A permanent lunar base is often described as a technical sequence: land, build, extract and expand. Yet a settlement is also a community, and communities need rules about privacy, care, leadership and access to scarce resources.

The first residents would not simply be pioneers. They would be workers operating inside a fragile system where a small failure could affect everyone. Their success would depend on cooperation as much as on machines designed to withstand radiation and dust.

Thinking about a lunar neighbourhood reveals a choice. We can reproduce the competitive logic of a frontier, or treat the settlement as an experiment in collective responsibility before its habits become permanent.
question: What broader issue does the lunar example raise?
options: Whether space travel can avoid using machines.
Whether new settlements should reproduce competition or practise collective responsibility.
Whether the Moon has enough dust for construction.
explanation: The final paragraph frames lunar settlement as a test of social models, not just engineering.

title: The grid that belongs to everyone
author: Aisha Bello
dek: Community solar turns energy production into a local relationship rather than a private rooftop privilege.
body: Not every household owns a roof suitable for solar panels. Community solar projects allow residents to subscribe to a shared installation and receive credit for the electricity it produces, widening access without requiring individual construction.

The model still depends on clear contracts, fair pricing and a grid capable of carrying power where it is needed. If those conditions are missing, the language of community can conceal a familiar pattern of unequal benefit.

Its promise lies in linking clean energy with participation. People become not only customers of a distant system, but members of a project whose advantages and responsibilities can be discussed locally.
question: What condition is necessary for community solar to be genuinely fair?
options: Every resident must own a private rooftop.
The project must combine shared access with transparent pricing and contracts.
Electricity must be produced only during daylight.
explanation: The writer warns that community language is insufficient without clear, fair arrangements.

title: The intelligence of learning by doing
author: Samuel Ortiz
dek: Apprenticeships develop judgement in the gap between knowing a rule and recognising when to apply it.
body: A manual can explain how to sharpen a tool, but it cannot list every sound that suggests a machine is under strain. Apprentices learn such distinctions by observing experienced workers, trying a task and receiving correction while the consequences remain manageable.
This form of education can be difficult to measure because its progress is often tacit. A learner may not be able to define a standard yet can recognise when a result is unsafe or unfinished.
The apprenticeship model is therefore valuable beyond trades. It treats expertise as situated judgement, built through feedback and participation rather than transferred as information alone.
question: What kind of knowledge does apprenticeship develop particularly well?
options: Knowledge that only exists in textbooks.
Situated judgement about when and how to apply a rule.
A way to avoid receiving feedback.
explanation: The reading distinguishes tacit, contextual judgement from information that can simply be read.

title: Maps are arguments
author: Lina Haddad
dek: A map looks objective because its choices are usually hidden behind clean lines and familiar symbols.
body: Every map leaves something out. A transit map may distort geography to clarify connections; a property map may make ownership appear more permanent than occupation; a colonial map may rename a landscape while presenting the new names as natural.
These distortions do not make maps useless. They reveal the purpose for which each map was made and the audience it expected to serve.

Reading a map critically means asking whose movement it supports, whose presence it records and whose knowledge it treats as authoritative. The image is not merely a picture of territory; it is an argument about how territory should be understood.
question: How does the writer recommend reading maps?
options: As perfectly neutral pictures of territory.
By examining the choices, purposes and authorities behind them.
By ignoring the labels used on them.
explanation: The writer calls maps arguments and asks readers to inspect their omissions and intended uses.

title: Libraries after the age of scarcity
author: Bea Williams
dek: When information is abundant, a library's value shifts from holding books to helping people navigate abundance.
body: A library once answered a practical problem: a community could not afford to own every book, so it shared a smaller collection. Digital access has changed that equation, but it has not removed the need for trusted selection, quiet space and human guidance.
Librarians now help visitors evaluate sources, use unfamiliar tools and find material that an algorithm may overlook because it is too local, old or difficult to classify.

The modern library is therefore less a warehouse of scarcity than an institution of attention. It creates conditions in which knowledge can be approached deliberately rather than consumed as an endless stream.
question: What role does the writer see for libraries today?
options: To compete with search engines by storing every webpage.
To provide trusted guidance and conditions for deliberate attention.
To make digital information less available.
explanation: The reading reframes the library as a guide and attentional environment in an age of abundance.

title: Farming below the waves
author: Noah Kim
dek: Underwater farms suggest that food production can make use of spaces that conventional agriculture leaves untouched.
body: In sheltered coastal waters, gardeners are cultivating seaweed, shellfish and herbs inside structures that need no freshwater or fertile soil. The farms can also provide habitat for small marine organisms, although their effects depend on scale and location.

The idea is attractive partly because it challenges a familiar assumption: that food must be grown on land. Still, the ocean is not an empty surface waiting for use. Navigation, fishing, conservation and local livelihoods all compete for the same space.
The real test is therefore governance. Underwater farming will be sustainable only if its ecological benefits are assessed alongside the rights and knowledge of coastal communities.
question: What does the writer identify as the main test for underwater farming?
options: Whether it can replace all land agriculture.
Whether it can be governed with ecological and community interests in mind.
Whether the ocean is an unused space.
explanation: The conclusion shifts attention from novelty to how competing coastal interests are governed.

title: A palace made of ordinary rooms
author: Eli Brooks
dek: Memory palaces work because imagination gives abstract information a place to live.
body: To build a memory palace, a learner imagines walking through a familiar building and places ideas along a route. A list of dates becomes a scene in a hallway; an argument waits beside a staircase. The method turns sequence into movement.

Its effectiveness does not depend on an exceptional visual imagination. What matters is the relationship between a stable structure and deliberately unusual images, which provide retrieval cues stronger than repetition alone.
The technique illustrates a wider principle of learning: information becomes easier to recall when it is connected to context, emotion or action rather than left as an isolated item.
question: Why can a memory palace improve recall?
options: It removes the need to understand information.
It connects abstract information to a stable context and vivid cues.
It works only for people with exceptional visual talent.
explanation: The method is described as adding context and retrieval cues to otherwise isolated information.

title: Care in the age of gentle machines
author: Jules Adeyemi
dek: Care robots may reduce physical strain, but they cannot make care a purely technical task.
body: A lifting robot can help a nurse move a patient; a reminder device can help someone living alone keep track of medication. These functions matter because repetitive physical work can exhaust the people who provide care.

However, care is also interpretation. A pause may signal fear, a refusal may be a request for control and a familiar story may be a way of asking to be heard. A machine can detect patterns, but it cannot decide what dignity should mean in a particular relationship.
The most responsible systems will therefore assist caregivers without replacing the human attention that gives care its moral content.
question: What boundary does the writer place on the use of care robots?
options: They should perform no physical tasks.
They may assist with strain but should not replace human interpretation and attention.
They should decide what dignity means for every patient.
explanation: The reading supports assistance while reserving relational and moral judgement for people.

title: The market for endangered habitats
author: Inez Harper
dek: Biodiversity credits try to make habitat protection financially visible, but measurement can never capture every ecological relationship.
body: A biodiversity credit may represent the restoration of a wetland or the protection of a forest. By attaching a value to improvement, the system hopes to attract funding that conservation projects often struggle to secure.

The danger is that a complex habitat becomes a number that can be traded far from the place where decisions are made. A credit may reward visible species while overlooking soil organisms, cultural uses or relationships that have not been measured.

Credits can be useful as one funding tool, but only if they remain subordinate to ecological limits and local stewardship. What is countable should not automatically become the definition of what matters.
question: What danger of biodiversity credits does the writer highlight?
options: They make conservation impossible to fund.
They may reduce complex ecological value to what can be measured and traded.
They always ignore visible species.
explanation: The writer warns that financial measurement can privilege countable features over less visible relationships.

title: Why grief needs a public language
author: Amara Cole
dek: Rituals do not remove loss, but they give a community a way to recognise that life has changed.
body: Modern life often treats grief as a private interruption to productivity. A person is expected to return to ordinary schedules while the meaning of ordinary things has shifted underneath them.
Public rituals create a shared vocabulary for what cannot be solved. A meal, a song or a walk to a familiar place allows people to witness one another without requiring a polished explanation.

Such practices are not identical across cultures, yet they share a purpose: they make absence socially visible. By doing so, they give the bereaved permission to belong to a community that has changed with them.
question: What function do grief rituals serve according to the writer?
options: They solve the practical problems caused by loss.
They make loss visible and give communities a shared way to acknowledge change.
They require everyone to grieve in the same manner.
explanation: The reading says rituals do not solve grief but help communities witness and recognise it.

title: The dust that returns to us
author: Ravi Das
dek: Microplastics are difficult to govern partly because they move through systems designed to separate responsibility.
body: Tiny plastic particles appear in water, soil, air and living tissue. They come from tyres, clothing, packaging and industrial processes, which means no single product or industry can claim to be the whole story.

Their scale creates a political problem. A filter may remove particles from one stream while production continues elsewhere, and a consumer can be blamed for a waste pattern built into the design of ordinary goods.

Effective action will require more than asking individuals to dispose of plastic carefully. It will mean redesigning materials, regulating production and treating pollution as a chain of decisions rather than a final-stage mistake.
question: Why is microplastic pollution difficult to govern?
options: It comes from one easily identifiable product.
It crosses systems and distributes responsibility across many decisions.
It exists only in water.
explanation: The writer emphasises multiple sources and a chain of responsibility from design to disposal.

title: The geography of a remote job
author: Felix Moore
dek: Remote work changes more than the location of a desk; it redistributes who can participate in a labour market.
body: A remote role may allow a parent to remain in a smaller town, or a specialist to work for a company that once required relocation. The benefit is real, but it is not evenly available: reliable internet, a quiet room and flexible management are forms of infrastructure.

Cities may lose some commuters while gaining residents who spend more time locally. Meanwhile, regions with lower living costs can become attractive to employers, raising questions about whether remote work will spread opportunity or simply move pressure elsewhere.
The geography of work is therefore being rewritten, not erased. Location still matters; it has merely become less visible in the contract.
question: What does the writer imply about remote work and geography?
options: Remote work makes location irrelevant.
Remote work redistributes opportunities but still depends on spatial infrastructure.
Only large cities can benefit from remote work.
explanation: The final line says location still matters, even when it is less visible in a remote contract.

title: When a coastline becomes a destination
author: Mariam Yusuf
dek: Climate migration is often discussed as movement, but the harder question is how belonging is negotiated after arrival.
body: When rising seas or repeated storms make a place unsafe, leaving can be described as a practical decision. For the people who move, however, the destination is not a blank page. It has its own history, housing pressures and ideas about who counts as local.

Successful adaptation requires more than building accommodation. New arrivals need access to schools, work and political voice, while existing residents need confidence that resources will not be distributed without consultation.
The language of climate migration should therefore include both emergency and membership. A safe landing is not simply a roof; it is the possibility of participating in a shared future.
question: What does the writer mean by a 'safe landing'?
options: Arrival in a place with no history or existing residents.
Access to housing plus the chance to participate in community life.
A temporary visit before returning home.
explanation: The conclusion defines safety as social and political participation as well as shelter.

title: The laboratory with an open door
author: Eva Chen
dek: Open-source science can widen participation, but shared tools still need standards, credit and care.
body: When researchers publish code, methods and data openly, other teams can reproduce an experiment or adapt it to a new question. The result is a scientific process that is easier to inspect and potentially faster to improve.
Openness does not remove the need for expertise. Poorly documented code can exclude newcomers, while uncredited contributions can discourage the very collaboration the model hopes to encourage.
An open laboratory is therefore not simply a folder of free files. It is a social arrangement that must make methods legible, contributions visible and disagreement productive.
question: What does open-source science require beyond free access?
options: Less expertise and fewer standards.
Documentation, fair credit and practices that support collaboration.
A ban on adapting existing experiments.
explanation: The reading distinguishes access from the social and technical conditions needed to make openness work.

title: The economics of an uneaten meal
author: Tessa Lin
dek: Food waste is not only a household habit; it is the visible end of a system that often rewards abundance over accuracy.
body: A supermarket may reject vegetables that look unusual, a restaurant may prepare more portions than it expects to sell and a family may buy in bulk because a discount makes surplus feel economical. At every stage, value is lost before food reaches a plate.

Telling consumers to plan better has some effect, but it can obscure the incentives built into the supply chain. Waste is frequently a rational response to uncertainty when shortage is punished more severely than excess.

Reducing it will require better forecasting, flexible standards and business models that value food before it becomes rubbish. Personal discipline matters, but design determines how hard discipline must work.
question: What systemic cause of food waste does the writer identify?
options: Consumers are the only people making wasteful decisions.
Supply chains often reward excess as protection against uncertainty.
Unusual-looking vegetables contain no nutritional value.
explanation: The writer explains that abundance and risk management can make surplus a rational system response.

title: The silence inside the archive
author: Josephine Reed
dek: An archive can preserve evidence while still reflecting the power structures that decided what was worth recording.
body: Researchers often begin with a box of letters, a register or a photograph and ask what it reveals. They must also ask what is absent: the voices of people who could not write, whose documents were discarded or whose lives were considered too ordinary to preserve.
Adding new material does not automatically repair this silence. A collection can become larger without changing the categories that determine which testimony is treated as authoritative.
Responsible archival work therefore involves both recovery and suspicion. It brings overlooked voices forward while remaining honest about the institutions that shaped the record in the first place.
question: Why must archival researchers ask what is absent?
options: Because every archive has been destroyed.
Because recording practices reflect power and can exclude ordinary or marginalised lives.
Because photographs are less useful than letters.
explanation: The writer argues that archives are shaped by decisions about whose lives deserved to be recorded.

title: The policy hidden in a good night's sleep
author: Mei Torres
dek: Sleep is often treated as an individual responsibility even though schedules and workplaces shape the conditions for rest.
body: Advice about sleep tends to focus on personal routines: reduce screens, keep a regular bedtime and make the bedroom calm. Those habits help, but they cannot fully compensate for night shifts, long commutes or institutions that reward availability at every hour.
A public-health view asks different questions. Can workers recover between shifts? Do schools begin early enough for adolescents? Does a city remain safe and quiet at night?
Sleep policy makes visible the social organisation behind a private feeling. Rest is personal, but the ability to obtain it is partly governed by other people's timetables.
question: What does a public-health view of sleep add?
options: It rejects all personal routines.
It examines how work, school and city schedules create or limit the conditions for rest.
It treats sleep as unrelated to institutions.
explanation: The writer expands the discussion from individual habits to the schedules and systems that shape them.

title: The button that wants to be pressed
author: Luca Stein
dek: Dark patterns are interfaces that use design knowledge to make a user's least reflective choice the easiest one.
body: A service may make the button to accept tracking bright and immediate while hiding the route to decline it behind several screens. The user technically has a choice, but the interface has already expressed which choice the company prefers.
Such design is persuasive rather than neutral. It exploits haste, uncertainty and visual habits that people rely on when they are trying to complete a task quickly.
Regulation can prohibit specific tricks, yet the deeper principle is broader: consent should be judged by the quality of the decision, not merely by the existence of a button that can be clicked.
question: What principle does the writer propose for judging consent?
options: Whether a button exists somewhere in the interface.
Whether the design allows a genuinely informed and reflective decision.
Whether the preferred company option is brightly coloured.
explanation: The conclusion focuses on the quality of consent, not the formal presence of a choice.

title: Who gets to save a seed?
author: Anika Rao
dek: Seed sovereignty links biodiversity to the right of communities to control the crops they depend on.
body: A seed bank can preserve genetic variety, but preservation in a distant institution is not the same as control in the field. Farmers who save and exchange seed maintain knowledge about soil, seasons and taste that cannot be stored in a freezer alone.

Commercial agriculture often favours uniform varieties because they simplify processing and distribution. Uniformity can increase efficiency while leaving crops more exposed to disease or climate shocks.

Seed sovereignty is therefore both ecological and political. It asks who owns the future of food and whether resilience should be designed only in laboratories or also cultivated through local practice.
question: Why is seed sovereignty both ecological and political?
options: Because seed banks are always privately owned.
Because it concerns biodiversity as well as who controls food-growing knowledge and resources.
Because farmers no longer need to understand soil.
explanation: The reading connects genetic resilience with community control over seed and agricultural knowledge.

title: The ethical life of a replica
author: Martin Cole
dek: A replica can teach more people about an object, but it also raises questions about authenticity and access.
body: A museum may display a high-quality copy while the original rests in controlled storage, travels to another country or remains too fragile for public light. Visitors can still learn from form and scale, even when the material history has been substituted.

The substitution becomes problematic when it is hidden. A replica presented as original invites trust under false conditions; a clearly labelled copy can instead make the museum's care and limits part of the story.
Authenticity is not only a property of materials. It is also a relationship of honesty between an institution and the people it asks to believe it.
question: When can a replica be ethically useful?
options: When it is presented as the original without explanation.
When it expands access and its status is made clear.
Only when no visitor can see it.
explanation: The writer accepts replicas when they widen access without disguising the substitution.

title: The politics of a bilingual sign
author: Carmen Lee
dek: A translated sign can be a practical aid, but it can also announce who a city imagines belongs there.
body: A street sign in two languages may help a visitor navigate, preserve a minority language or acknowledge a contested history. The same sign can be criticised as symbolic decoration if public services remain unavailable in the language it displays.
Language policy is therefore judged in ordinary moments: at a clinic, on a school form or during an emergency. Visibility matters, but visibility without access can turn recognition into performance.

The most meaningful bilingual design connects words on walls to institutions that are prepared to listen. A sign is a promise about belonging; its credibility depends on what happens after it is read.
question: What makes bilingual signage more than symbolic decoration?
options: Using the largest possible letters.
Connecting visible recognition with real access to services and participation.
Displaying two languages only at tourist attractions.
explanation: The reading says signage becomes credible when institutions also provide practical language access.

title: Why constraints can make room for creativity
author: Tariq Moss
dek: A limit can feel restrictive, yet it can also reduce the paralysis created by unlimited possibilities.
body: A writer given an entirely open page may spend an hour wondering what the work ought to become. A form, a deadline or a small set of materials can narrow the field enough for attention to move from choosing a direction to exploring it.
Constraints are not automatically productive. A rule imposed without purpose can flatten experimentation, while a useful boundary gives a creative decision something to push against.
The value of a constraint lies in the conversation it creates between intention and resistance. It does not guarantee originality, but it can make sustained play easier to begin.
question: What is the writer's view of creative constraints?
options: They always prevent experimentation.
They can focus attention when unlimited choice becomes paralysing.
They guarantee that every result will be original.
explanation: The text presents constraints as potentially useful boundaries, not automatic guarantees of creativity.

title: Citizens in the room
author: Helena Ward
dek: Citizen assemblies can improve public decisions by giving disagreement time, structure and evidence.
body: A citizens' assembly brings together a group selected to reflect a wider population, then gives its members time to learn about an issue before discussing recommendations. The method is slower than a poll and less theatrical than a debate.

Its legitimacy depends on design. Participants need balanced information, skilled facilitation and a clear account of how their conclusions will influence policy. Without these, an assembly can become a performance of consultation rather than a transfer of judgement.

When properly supported, the process changes the role of disagreement. It asks people not merely to express an identity, but to revise a view in response to reasons offered by people unlike themselves.
question: What makes a citizens' assembly legitimate?
options: A large audience and a quick vote.
Representative participation, balanced information and a clear policy connection.
The absence of disagreement between participants.
explanation: The writer lists design conditions that connect participation with accountable decision-making.

title: The promise and drag of electric flight
author: Nikhil Arun
dek: Electric aviation may transform short routes, but physics limits how quickly the transformation can scale.
body: Electric motors are efficient and quiet, which makes them attractive for regional aircraft. The obstacle is the weight of batteries: unlike fuel, they do not become lighter as energy is consumed, and current cells store less energy per kilogram.

This difference shapes the likely future. Small planes may become viable on short routes before large aircraft can cross oceans, while infrastructure and battery recycling will create further constraints.

The technology should therefore be judged neither as a miracle nor a failure. Its significance lies in the routes it can make cleaner, provided ambition follows the physical limits rather than marketing language.
question: Why does the writer expect electric aviation to begin with short routes?
options: Electric motors are less efficient in small aircraft.
Battery weight makes long-distance flight more demanding.
Short routes require no infrastructure.
explanation: Battery energy density and weight are identified as the main physical constraints on range.

title: The meeting that should have been a note
author: Laura Stein
dek: Meetings multiply when organisations confuse visibility with progress.
body: A meeting can create shared understanding when a problem is ambiguous or a decision genuinely requires several perspectives. It becomes wasteful when participants are invited mainly so that no one feels excluded from information that could have been written down.
The distinction is cultural. Some organisations reward the appearance of collaboration, while others make it safe to decline a meeting and ask for a concise record instead.
A useful calendar is not empty, but intentional. It protects live conversation for work that benefits from human presence and treats documentation as a form of respect for everyone else's attention.
question: When does the writer consider a meeting useful?
options: Whenever many people can be invited.
When ambiguity or a shared decision genuinely benefits from several perspectives.
When written information would be shorter.
explanation: The reading reserves meetings for problems that need live, multi-perspective discussion.

title: The value of a dark sky
author: Nora Bell
dek: Reducing artificial light can restore more than a view of the stars; it can repair ecological rhythms.
body: Street lighting helps people navigate at night, but excessive brightness also changes the behaviour of insects, birds and nocturnal mammals. It can interrupt migration, confuse predators and alter the timing of feeding.

A dark-sky policy does not mean leaving streets unsafe. Shielded fixtures, warmer colours and lights that dim when they are not needed can preserve visibility while limiting spill into habitats and bedrooms.
The night sky is therefore a shared environment. Protecting darkness asks cities to treat absence of light as a resource with ecological and cultural value.
question: What does a dark-sky policy aim to balance?
options: Complete darkness with no public lighting.
Human safety and navigation with ecological and cultural needs.
Tourism with the cost of building taller streets.
explanation: The text proposes smarter lighting rather than removing all illumination.

title: The argument under the pavement
author: Rosa Malik
dek: Water rights become visible when a resource treated as infinite reveals its limits.
body: A reservoir can make water seem like a simple service: turn a tap and supply appears. During drought, however, the hidden negotiations emerge. Farmers, cities, ecosystems and future residents all claim a share of a resource that cannot satisfy every demand at once.
The language of ownership can obscure dependence. A river is not only an input for one industry; it is also habitat, cultural memory and a system whose health affects people downstream.
Fair water policy must therefore distribute scarcity transparently and include voices that markets tend to overlook. The question is not only who paid for water, but who is affected when it is gone.
question: Why does the writer challenge the language of water ownership?
options: Because water has no economic value.
Because ownership can hide the shared ecological and social effects of use.
Because drought affects only farmers.
explanation: The reading presents water as a connected system whose effects cross the boundaries of ownership.

title: The tutor that never gets tired
author: June Okoro
dek: AI tutors can provide endless practice, but learning still depends on the quality of the questions a learner is helped to ask.
body: An AI tutor can rephrase an explanation, generate examples and respond at midnight. This availability is valuable for learners who lack immediate access to a teacher, especially when embarrassment would otherwise prevent them from asking for repetition.

Yet an answer that arrives instantly can close inquiry as easily as it supports it. A learner may accept a fluent explanation without testing its assumptions or noticing that the system has misunderstood the task.
The strongest tutors will not simply imitate certainty. They will prompt learners to explain their reasoning, identify ambiguity and recognise when human expertise is needed.
question: What does the writer expect from a strong AI tutor?
options: It should always answer immediately and confidently.
It should encourage reasoning and signal ambiguity rather than merely producing answers.
It should prevent learners from asking for repetition.
explanation: The conclusion values metacognition and appropriate uncertainty over fluent answers alone.

title: The bee on the balcony
author: Marta Green
dek: Urban pollinators thrive when cities offer a connected patchwork rather than a few isolated gardens.
body: A flowering balcony may attract bees, but a single patch cannot sustain them if the next source of nectar is too far away. Pollinators need a sequence of habitats that carries them through changing seasons.
This makes urban biodiversity a coordination problem. Window boxes, schoolyards, railway edges and public parks may belong to different owners, yet together they can form a corridor.
The most effective action is not always a spectacular garden. It is the quiet agreement to plant, cut and manage small spaces in ways that allow other living things to move through the city.
question: Why are connected habitats important for urban pollinators?
options: Bees can live on one balcony all year.
A sequence of small habitats can provide resources across seasons and distances.
Public parks are the only useful urban habitat.
explanation: The writer describes pollinator survival as depending on a connected seasonal corridor.

title: What resilience leaves out
author: David Imani
dek: A community can appear resilient in a crisis while quietly exhausting the people who keep it functioning.
body: Resilience is often measured by whether a service continues after a shock. That measure is useful, but incomplete. A school may stay open because staff work unpaid hours; a family may cope because one member abandons their own health.

Counting outcomes without counting effort turns endurance into a virtue that institutions can exploit. It rewards systems for surviving through hidden sacrifice instead of reducing the reasons sacrifice was needed.
A fuller account of resilience asks not only whether a community recovered, but who carried the burden and whether the recovery made future strain less likely.
question: What weakness in common measures of resilience does the writer identify?
options: They focus too much on future planning.
They can hide the unequal and exhausting labour behind apparent recovery.
They ignore whether services continue at all.
explanation: The reading warns that outcome-based measures may conceal who pays the human cost of endurance.

title: The politics of a public bench
author: Sienna Frost
dek: A bench seems neutral until its shape reveals who is expected to sit, rest or leave.
body: A comfortable bench with a back and shade welcomes an older person, a parent or someone waiting for a bus. A divided seat with an angled surface may discourage lying down, presenting exclusion as a minor design detail.

No single object explains a city's treatment of vulnerable people, but objects repeat values at street level. They can offer support without asking for payment, or make public space conditional on constant movement.
Design is never the whole policy, yet it tells residents what kinds of bodies and behaviours a place has imagined. A bench is small; the civic message attached to it is not.
question: What broader message can public furniture communicate?
options: That all public space is free from policy.
Which bodies and behaviours a city considers welcome.
That benches are more important than housing.
explanation: The writer uses the bench to show how repeated design choices communicate inclusion or exclusion.

title: The privacy setting nobody reads
author: Tomas Vale
dek: Privacy by design treats data protection as part of a product's structure, not a favour users must remember to request.
body: A long privacy policy may explain what a service does, but explanation after the fact is weaker than a system built to collect less in the first place. Defaults, retention periods and access controls decide how much trust a user is being asked to supply.

Designing for privacy can involve friction: a feature may be less personalised, or a company may have fewer data points to sell. That trade-off is real, but it should be visible rather than hidden behind the language of convenience.
Privacy by design does not promise zero risk. It changes responsibility by making restraint a technical requirement instead of an individual act of vigilance.
question: How does privacy by design change responsibility?
options: It makes users read longer policies every day.
It builds restraint into the system instead of relying only on user vigilance.
It removes every possible risk from a service.
explanation: The final paragraph explicitly contrasts structural restraint with individual vigilance.

title: A coral garden with a human gardener
author: Dina Okafor
dek: Coral restoration is a race against time, but planting fragments is only one part of rebuilding a reef.
body: Restoration teams grow coral fragments in nurseries and attach them to damaged sections of reef. The work can accelerate recovery, yet a reef is more than a collection of corals: fish, currents, temperature and water quality determine whether a new colony survives.
This makes restoration both hopeful and uncomfortable. It offers a visible action while reminding us that no nursery can compensate indefinitely for warming seas or polluted runoff.
The most credible projects combine local planting with pressure on the conditions that caused the damage. Repair matters, but prevention decides whether repair has a future.
question: What does the writer say coral restoration cannot do alone?
options: Grow any coral fragments in nurseries.
Compensate indefinitely for warming seas and polluted water.
Involve local communities.
explanation: The text says planting is useful but cannot replace action on the wider causes of reef decline.

title: The numbers behind the beautiful game
author: Evan Laurent
dek: Sports analytics can reveal patterns that intuition misses, but a metric never plays the match by itself.
body: A model can show that a midfielder creates space without touching the ball, or that a team concedes more often after a certain substitution. These insights challenge the habit of judging performance only through visible moments.

Numbers become dangerous when they are treated as complete explanations. A player may take a low-probability shot because the alternative was worse, or because a tactical instruction changed what the model can see.
Analytics is most useful when it creates better questions for coaches, not when it replaces judgement. The game remains a social system in which context is part of performance.
question: What is the writer's recommended role for sports analytics?
options: To replace coaches' judgement entirely.
To reveal patterns and generate better questions about performance.
To measure only visible actions such as shots.
explanation: The reading supports analytics as a tool for inquiry while warning against treating metrics as complete explanations.

title: The rhetoric of risk
author: Mariam Foster
dek: Calling something a risk can clarify uncertainty, but it can also make a political choice sound like a natural fact.
body: A government may describe a housing project as risky because costs are uncertain, while residents describe inaction as the greater danger. Both statements use the language of risk, yet they place attention on different futures and different groups.
Risk is never only a probability. It is also a question of whose losses count, who receives protection and who is expected to tolerate uncertainty.
Listening carefully to risk language can reveal hidden priorities. The task is not to remove uncertainty from public decisions, but to make the distribution of uncertainty discussable.
question: What can risk language conceal?
options: That probabilities can be calculated.
The political choices about whose losses and uncertainty are prioritised.
That public decisions involve any future consequences.
explanation: The writer argues that risk framing can hide distributional and political choices behind neutral-sounding language.

title: Products designed to survive us
author: Ari Cohen
dek: Repairable products ask manufacturers to design for a relationship that continues after the first sale.
body: A sealed device can be thinner and harder to tamper with, but its failure may turn the entire object into waste. A repairable design makes parts accessible, instructions available and replacement possible without specialised permission.
Manufacturers worry that longer product lives reduce repeat sales. Yet repair can build trust, create local work and shift competition towards durability rather than constant novelty.
The real change is cultural as well as technical. A product is no longer treated as finished at purchase; it becomes part of an ongoing relationship between maker, owner and material.
question: What wider change does repairable design encourage?
options: It makes products disposable sooner.
It shifts value from novelty and replacement toward durability and ongoing relationships.
It prevents local repair work from developing.
explanation: The reading presents repairability as a change in how products, sales and ownership are understood.

title: Voices that do not fit a spreadsheet
author: Iris Mensah
dek: Oral history preserves knowledge that formal records often flatten or exclude.
body: An interview can hold pauses, contradictions and details that a report would remove in the name of clarity. A resident may remember a demolished street through the smell of a bakery, or describe a policy through the workaround it forced on a family.
Such testimony is not automatically objective. Memory changes, interviews are shaped by the questions asked and a recorded voice can still be edited or taken out of context.
Its value lies elsewhere. Oral history makes lived interpretation part of the archive and allows communities to name experiences that official categories were never designed to capture.
question: What is the distinctive value of oral history?
options: It provides a perfectly objective record.
It preserves lived interpretations that official categories may exclude.
It eliminates the need for careful interviewing.
explanation: The writer acknowledges limits but values oral history for making lived experience archivable.

title: The colleague who knows when to ask
author: Grace Ito
dek: Workplace friendship is not simply social comfort; it can create the trust needed for honest information to travel.
body: A colleague who knows your habits may notice when a confident presentation hides uncertainty. That knowledge can make it easier to ask a difficult question before a small problem becomes a public failure.

Friendship at work has risks: informal networks can exclude people and personal loyalty can blur professional judgement. The answer is not to remove relationships, but to prevent them becoming the only route to influence.
Healthy workplaces allow trust to spread beyond private friendships. They use the safety created by relationships to build better systems, not to make belonging dependent on having the right ally.
question: What distinction does the writer make about workplace friendship?
options: It is always harmful to professional judgement.
It can support honest communication but must not become the only route to influence.
It should replace formal workplace systems.
explanation: The reading recognises friendship's benefits while warning against informal exclusion.

title: The city inside the data centre
author: Samira Khan
dek: Cloud services feel weightless, yet the infrastructure behind them consumes land, water and electricity.
body: A data centre can seem like a neutral box on the edge of a city. Inside, however, servers generate heat that must be removed, power must be secured and equipment must be replaced as demand grows.

The services it supports may reduce travel or make small organisations more efficient, but those benefits do not erase local costs. A facility can compete for water during drought or connect a region to an industry whose jobs are limited.
The cloud is therefore a physical geography. Responsible planning asks not only what digital access enables, but which communities host the conditions that make it possible.
question: What does the writer mean by calling the cloud a physical geography?
options: Cloud services exist only in the sky.
Digital services depend on material infrastructure with local environmental and social effects.
Data centres never use water or electricity.
explanation: The text makes the physical resource demands and host-community effects of data centres visible.

title: Soil that remembers carbon
author: Lena Ford
dek: Soil carbon programmes can reward better farming, but measuring improvement is harder than promising it.
body: Plants move carbon from the atmosphere into soil through roots and organic matter. Farming practices such as cover crops and reduced disturbance may increase that storage, while drought or a later change in management can reverse it.

This uncertainty complicates carbon markets. A farmer may follow a careful plan for years, yet the climate benefit depends on conditions beyond a single harvest and on measurements that are expensive to repeat.
Soil carbon can still be valuable, especially when payments support healthier land. It should be treated as a long-term ecological practice rather than a simple certificate of permanent removal.
question: Why is soil carbon difficult to treat as a simple certificate?
options: Soil cannot contain organic matter.
Storage can change over time and depends on management and measurement.
Farmers never change their practices.
explanation: The writer highlights reversal, measurement costs and long-term dependence on management.

title: The price of a fair cup
author: Eliot Brown
dek: Fair-trade labels promise a better chain, but fairness cannot be reduced to a logo on the final product.
body: A coffee label may guarantee a minimum price or require certain labour standards. Those rules can protect farmers from the worst volatility, but they do not automatically redistribute power across a supply chain shaped by climate, debt and unequal bargaining.

Consumers often want a clear moral choice, while producers need flexible support that reflects local costs and risks. A single label can simplify a complex relationship that remains dependent on buyers' behaviour.

Fairness is therefore a practice of accountability rather than a permanent property of packaging. It requires transparency, producer voice and a willingness to examine who still has room to negotiate.
question: What does the writer say a fair-trade label cannot guarantee by itself?
options: That coffee has a price.
That power and bargaining are fairly distributed throughout the supply chain.
That consumers can read the packaging.
explanation: The reading says a label may help but cannot alone rebalance deeper power relationships.

title: Prediction and the policing of futures
author: Jonas Reed
dek: A predictive system can turn historical inequality into an apparently objective forecast.
body: If a neighbourhood has been over-policed, its records may contain more incidents than a similar neighbourhood that received less surveillance. A model trained on those records can then identify the first area as higher risk, creating a feedback loop that appears to confirm itself.
The problem is not only biased data. It is the belief that a prediction describes a future independent of the intervention used to produce it.
Responsible public systems must examine what their data measures, who is affected by errors and whether prediction is being used to allocate care or to justify greater suspicion.
question: How can predictive policing create a feedback loop?
options: It ignores all historical records.
Past unequal surveillance produces data that directs more surveillance to the same places.
It guarantees that every prediction is correct.
explanation: The first paragraph describes how unequal past policing can be mistaken for neutral evidence of future risk.

title: Mapping how a place feels
author: Amina Wells
dek: Emotional maps add human experience to the physical coordinates that conventional planning prefers.
body: A standard map can locate a crossing, a bus stop and a park. It cannot easily show where people feel watched, hurried, welcome or unable to rest. Community mapping projects ask residents to record these sensations alongside the physical features that produce them.

Such maps are subjective by design. Their purpose is not to replace traffic data, but to reveal why a technically efficient route may remain unused or why a small change in lighting can alter a street's meaning.
When planning includes feeling, the city becomes more than a network of movement. It becomes an environment whose success is partly measured by the quality of experience it makes possible.
question: What can emotional maps reveal that conventional maps may miss?
options: The exact number of bus stops.
How physical features shape feelings of safety, belonging or pressure.
A completely objective account of every resident's experience.
explanation: The reading uses emotional mapping to make subjective experience part of planning evidence.

title: The inheritance nobody sees
author: Sora Williams
dek: Inherited wealth includes more than money; it can include time, confidence and a margin for failure.
body: A young adult whose family can offer a deposit receives an obvious financial advantage. Less visible are the unpaid childcare, introductions and freedom to take an unpaid internship that may shape a career just as strongly.

Merit-based stories often celebrate the final effort while ignoring the cushion that made experimentation possible. This does not make achievement unreal, but it makes comparisons between people less simple than they appear.
A fairer discussion of opportunity must therefore count the resources that sit behind individual choices. Equality is not achieved by praising effort while leaving the starting conditions unnamed.
question: What forms of inheritance does the writer want readers to notice?
options: Only money held in a bank account.
Financial and social resources such as time, connections and room to take risks.
The belief that effort never matters.
explanation: The reading expands inheritance to include support and flexibility that make choices possible.

title: The default setting is a decision
author: Milo Grant
dek: Defaults feel invisible because they remove a choice, yet that removal often shapes behaviour more powerfully than advice.
body: A pension plan that enrols workers automatically can raise participation because opting out requires a deliberate act. The same mechanism can also keep people subscribed to a service or sharing more data than they intended.

Defaults are not neutral, but neither are they automatically manipulative. Their ethical status depends on the stakes, the ease of changing them and whether the setting serves the person's interests or the institution's convenience.
Designers should therefore treat every default as a policy decision. The question is not whether people clicked, but why the system chose one path to be effortless.
question: What determines whether a default is ethically acceptable?
options: Whether users notice it immediately.
The stakes, reversibility and interests served by the default.
Whether the default is impossible to change.
explanation: The writer evaluates defaults by consequence, reversibility and whose interests the design advances.

title: A sculpture that changes its neighbours
author: Priyanka Shah
dek: Public art does not simply occupy space; it can alter how residents notice and argue about a place.
body: A sculpture in a square may become a landmark, a meeting point or a source of dispute. Its meaning changes as people use the surrounding space, photograph it and attach memories to its form.

Commissioning public art is therefore not only a question of taste. It can shift foot traffic, increase attention to an overlooked area or signal that a community's history is being celebrated—or contested.

The work succeeds when it remains open to public interpretation without pretending that every interpretation carries equal power. Art can invite conversation, but the conversation still depends on who is allowed to shape the square around it.
question: Why does the writer treat public art as more than decoration?
options: Because it always avoids controversy.
Because it can change how a place is used, remembered and contested.
Because it controls every interpretation of a square.
explanation: The reading links public art to movement, memory and power in shared space.

title: The danger of a single crop
author: Rina Patel
dek: Agricultural monoculture can simplify production while making a food system less able to absorb shock.
body: When large areas grow the same variety, harvesting and processing become efficient. The uniform crop is also exposed to a shared weakness: a pest, disease or heat event can move through it without meeting much resistance.
Diversity makes farming more complicated because different crops need different timing and care. It can, however, spread risk across species, soil conditions and markets.
The argument for diversity is not nostalgia for a simpler past. It is an argument for flexibility in a future where climate and disease make certainty increasingly expensive.
question: Why can crop diversity increase resilience?
options: It makes all farming tasks identical.
It spreads risk across different species, conditions and markets.
It guarantees that no crop will ever fail.
explanation: The text contrasts a shared vulnerability with the risk-spreading function of diversity.

title: The house that negotiates
author: Omar Ruiz
dek: A smart home can save energy, but automation also decides what comfort should mean.
body: A thermostat that learns routines may reduce heating when a room is empty. Sensors can adjust light or suggest when an appliance is consuming unusually much power, making efficiency less dependent on constant attention.
The same system may misread irregular lives. A guest, a night worker or someone with a fluctuating health condition can be treated as an anomaly that the algorithm should correct.
Smartness is therefore not simply responsiveness. It is a negotiation between prediction and permission, and the resident should remain able to understand and override the choices being made.
question: What condition does the writer place on a genuinely smart home?
options: It must remove all manual controls.
It should make understandable choices while allowing residents to override them.
It should assume every resident follows the same routine.
explanation: The conclusion values transparency and control over automation that silently corrects people.

title: The long route of a migrating bird
author: Hugo Stein
dek: Protecting migratory birds requires cooperation across places that may never share the same season.
body: A bird can depend on a wetland in one country, a coastline in another and a small patch of forest thousands of kilometres away. Protecting only one stop leaves the route vulnerable to a decision made elsewhere.
Migration makes ecology politically awkward because responsibility is distributed across borders and time. The people who benefit from a wetland's presence may not be the people asked to protect it.
International agreements matter, but so do local relationships with land. A migration route survives when its many temporary homes are treated as one connected system.
question: Why is protecting migratory birds politically difficult?
options: Birds never use more than one habitat.
Their survival depends on multiple jurisdictions with different responsibilities.
Migration occurs only within one country.
explanation: The reading emphasises that a single animal's route crosses many places and decision-makers.

title: The mechanics of an apology
author: Nadia Ali
dek: An apology repairs trust only when it names harm without asking the harmed person to manage the repair.
body: A public statement can contain regret while avoiding responsibility: the speaker may focus on intention, describe the outcome as misunderstood or move quickly to a promise of improvement. Such language protects the speaker from discomfort but leaves the original harm unnamed.
A meaningful apology is more specific. It identifies what happened, recognises its effect and accepts that the person harmed may need time rather than immediate reconciliation.
Apology is therefore an action with consequences, not a performance of humility. Its credibility appears in what changes after the words have been spoken.
question: What makes an apology credible according to the writer?
