options: It focuses on the speaker's good intentions.
It names the harm, recognises its effect and is followed by change.
It demands immediate forgiveness.
explanation: The text defines credibility through specificity, recognition and consequences rather than emotion alone.

title: The future imagined by climate fiction
author: Rosa Green
dek: Climate fiction does not predict one future; it enlarges the range of futures people can emotionally imagine.
body: A scientific report can describe temperature, sea level and probability. A novel can place a reader inside a family argument during a blackout or make a changed coastline feel like a personal loss rather than a distant statistic.

This emotional work is not a substitute for evidence. Its value is different: it challenges the assumption that the future is too abstract to rehearse and can reveal whose choices become visible when conditions change.
Climate fiction matters when it refuses both easy catastrophe and easy hope. By making futures inhabitable in imagination, it can make present decisions feel less inevitable.
question: What distinctive role does climate fiction play?
options: It replaces scientific evidence with prediction.
It makes possible futures emotionally and socially imaginable.
It guarantees that readers will agree about climate policy.
explanation: The reading distinguishes fiction's emotional and imaginative contribution from scientific prediction.

title: A library card is a key
author: Bea Williams
dek: A public library offers more than books: it offers a safe place to look for answers.
body: People visit libraries to borrow novels, use computers or find a quiet table. For someone without a reliable internet connection or a calm room at home, these simple services can make learning and work possible.
The librarian can also help a visitor choose a trustworthy source or discover a book they would not have found alone. In this way, the library connects people with both information and confidence.
A library card may look small, but it opens a shared door to knowledge, space and community.
question: What does the library provide besides books?
options: Only a place to buy computers.
Access to space, technology, guidance and information.
A private room for every visitor.
explanation: The reading lists computers, quiet space, guidance and information as library benefits.

title: Why protected bike lanes matter
author: Evan Laurent
dek: A safe bike lane can change who feels able to travel across a city.
body: Many people like the idea of cycling, but they do not want to ride beside fast traffic. A protected lane gives cyclists a separate space and makes the journey feel less risky.
When more people cycle, streets can become quieter and buses may face less traffic. Shops can also benefit when people stop more easily along a route.
A bike lane is therefore not only for confident cyclists. Good design can invite children, older people and beginners to use a different way of moving.
question: Who can benefit from a well-designed protected bike lane?
options: Only professional cyclists.
A wider range of people, including beginners and older residents.
Only people who own cars.
explanation: The final paragraph specifically names beginners, children and older people.

title: The story inside a cup of coffee
author: Eliot Brown
dek: Coffee connects farms, transport, shops and daily routines across many countries.
body: A cup of coffee begins with a plant grown by a farmer, but its journey continues through roasting, shipping and preparation. Each stage adds work and cost before the drink reaches a customer.

The price on a menu does not always show how much the farmer earned or how much the environment was affected. Learning about the chain can help customers ask better questions about the products they buy.
A simple drink can therefore tell a much bigger story about trade and responsibility.
question: Why might the price of coffee be incomplete information?
options: Because coffee has no stages before it reaches a café.
Because it may not show how value and responsibility are shared along the chain.
Because coffee is always free for farmers.
explanation: The reading says the menu price does not fully reveal farmers' earnings or environmental effects.

title: Listening to a museum
author: Clara Voss
dek: An audio guide can change a museum visit from looking at objects to following a story.
body: Some visitors enjoy reading labels, while others learn better when they hear a voice explain an object. An audio guide can give background, pronunciation and a suggested route through a large exhibition.
It can also influence what visitors notice. A guide may point to a small detail that would otherwise be missed, although it may leave less room for a personal interpretation.
The best audio guides offer information without telling visitors exactly what they must feel.
question: What is one possible disadvantage of an audio guide?
options: It can make every object disappear.
It may leave less space for a visitor's own interpretation.
It cannot explain pronunciation.
explanation: The second paragraph notes that a guide can direct attention so strongly that personal interpretation is reduced.

title: A garden between buildings
author: Marta Green
dek: A small community garden can improve a neighbourhood's food, air and social life.
body: Residents can grow herbs and vegetables in a garden built on an empty piece of land. People who do not have a private garden can learn how to plant and share the harvest.
The garden also creates a place to meet. Neighbours may talk while watering plants, and children can see where food comes from.
It will not feed an entire city, but it can make one small area greener, friendlier and more connected.
question: What is one social benefit of a community garden?
options: It gives every resident a private farm.
It creates a place where neighbours can meet and learn together.
It removes the need for all city parks.
explanation: The reading describes people meeting while caring for the shared garden.

title: Why idioms are tricky
author: Nora Ellis
dek: Idioms make language lively, but their meaning cannot always be understood word by word.
body: If someone says they are feeling under the weather, they usually do not mean that they are physically below a cloud. The phrase means that they feel unwell.
Idioms often come from history, work or everyday objects. Learners understand them more easily when they see the phrase in a real situation and notice how native speakers use it.
Learning an idiom is therefore like learning a small piece of culture as well as a new group of words.
question: Why can idioms be difficult for learners?
options: Their meaning is always exactly literal.
Their meaning may not be clear from the individual words.
They are used only in written exams.
explanation: The example shows that the literal words do not explain the intended meaning.

title: Feedback that helps students move
author: June Okoro
dek: Useful feedback tells a learner what to try next, not only what went wrong.
body: A comment such as good job may feel pleasant, but it does not tell a student how to improve. A more useful comment identifies one successful choice and gives a clear suggestion for the next attempt.
Feedback also works best when it arrives while the task is still fresh. If a student understands the reason for a correction, they can use the idea in a new context.
The aim is not to fill a page with criticism. It is to give the learner a practical next step.
question: What should useful feedback include?
options: Only praise with no advice.
A clear next step based on what the learner did.
A long list of every possible mistake.
explanation: The reading defines useful feedback as specific, timely and focused on a practical next step.

title: The bus stop as a small classroom
author: Lina Haddad
dek: A well-designed bus stop can make waiting safer, calmer and more informative.
body: A shelter protects passengers from rain and sun, while a clear timetable helps them plan. Seating is especially important for people who cannot stand for long periods.
Some cities add real-time information or a small light powered by solar energy. These details do not make the bus arrive sooner, but they reduce uncertainty while people wait.
The bus stop shows that transport includes the time before and after the vehicle moves.
question: How can a bus stop reduce uncertainty?
options: By making buses travel faster in every case.
By giving passengers clear timetable or real-time information.
By removing all seating.
explanation: The reading names timetables and real-time information as tools that reduce uncertainty.

title: A guide to watching meteor showers
author: Owen Price
dek: Watching a meteor shower is easier when patience is treated as part of the activity.
body: Meteors are easiest to see away from bright city lights. Observers should let their eyes adjust, choose a wide view of the sky and avoid looking at a phone too often.
A meteor shower does not produce a constant display. There may be several quiet minutes followed by a brief bright line, so comfort and patience matter.
The reward is not only the number of meteors seen. It is also the feeling of noticing movement in a sky that usually seems still.
question: Why should observers avoid looking at a phone often?
options: A phone makes meteors move faster.
Bright screen light can make it harder for their eyes to adjust to darkness.
Phones can only be used indoors.
explanation: The advice assumes that screen light interferes with dark adaptation.

title: Cooking as a transferable skill
author: Mina Laurent
dek: Cooking teaches more than recipes; it develops planning, observation and flexibility.
body: A recipe gives a useful starting point, but ingredients vary. A tomato may contain more water than expected, or a pan may heat faster than the instructions suggest.
Good cooks learn to observe texture, smell and timing instead of following every number blindly. They also learn how to replace an ingredient when a shop or budget creates a limitation.
These skills transfer beyond the kitchen: plan ahead, notice change and adjust without abandoning the goal.
question: What does cooking teach beyond following a recipe?
options: That ingredients never vary.
How to observe conditions and adapt while keeping a goal in mind.
That planning is unnecessary.
explanation: The text highlights observation, substitution and adaptation as skills developed through cooking.

title: The quiet mathematics of volunteering
author: Amara Cole
dek: Volunteering produces benefits that are not always visible in a simple count of hours.
body: A volunteer may sort food, visit an older person or help a child practise reading. The task has a direct result, but it can also create trust and make a person feel less alone.
These effects are difficult to measure because they happen slowly and through relationships. A short conversation may matter more than a completed list of tasks.
Volunteering is therefore both practical work and social connection.
question: Why are the benefits of volunteering difficult to measure?
options: Volunteers never complete practical tasks.
Some benefits, such as trust and reduced loneliness, develop through relationships.
Only paid work has visible results.
explanation: The reading contrasts measurable tasks with slower relational benefits.

title: Reading on paper and on screen
author: Eli Brooks
dek: The best reading format depends partly on the kind of attention a text requires.
body: A screen is convenient for searching, changing the size of words and carrying many books. Paper can make it easier for some readers to remember where an idea appeared on a page.
Neither format guarantees deep reading. Notifications can interrupt a screen, while a paper book can also be ignored if the reader is tired or distracted.
Choosing a format should begin with the purpose: quick reference, careful study or pleasure.
question: What does the writer recommend when choosing a reading format?
options: Always choosing paper.
Considering the purpose and the kind of attention needed.
Always choosing the most expensive device.
explanation: The conclusion says format should follow purpose rather than a universal rule.

title: A park with many jobs
author: Sienna Frost
dek: A city park can support exercise, wildlife, play and rest at the same time.
body: One part of a park may have trees for birds, while another has a path for runners and a bench for people who want to sit. Good planning allows different activities without making one group feel that it owns the whole space.
The park also changes with the seasons. A quiet winter path may become a busy picnic area in summer.
Its success is not measured by one use, but by how many forms of life and activity it can support.
question: What makes a city park successful in the reading?
options: It allows only one activity.
It supports different people, activities and seasons.
It has no plants or quiet areas.
explanation: The reading values a park's ability to accommodate varied uses and seasonal change.

title: The question behind the interview question
author: Marcus Green
dek: A job interview is often a test of examples, but it is also a conversation about how someone thinks.
body: When an interviewer asks about a difficult project, they may want to hear the result, but they also want to know how the candidate understood the problem and what they learned.
A strong answer is specific without becoming a long story. It explains the situation, the action taken and the reason behind that action.
Preparing examples can make an interview calmer because the candidate is ready to discuss decisions rather than memorise perfect sentences.
question: What should a strong interview answer explain?
options: Only the final result.
The situation, the action taken and the reasoning behind it.
A perfect sentence with no example.
explanation: The second paragraph gives the three elements of a useful answer.

title: Recycling is not the whole answer
author: Ravi Das
dek: Recycling can help, but reducing and reusing materials usually avoid more waste in the first place.
body: Recycling turns some used materials into new products, which can reduce the need for new resources. However, not every item can be recycled, and collection systems differ from one place to another.
Buying less, choosing durable products and repairing broken objects prevent waste before it exists. These actions can be harder when products are cheap and designed to be replaced.
Recycling matters, but it should be one part of a wider approach to materials.
question: What should accompany recycling?
options: Buying more disposable products.
Reducing consumption, reusing items and repairing them where possible.
Ignoring local collection rules.
explanation: The reading places recycling within a wider hierarchy that includes reduction, reuse and repair.

title: A language exchange that works
author: Yuki Sato
dek: A language exchange is most useful when both speakers have time to practise and correct one another kindly.
body: Two people can meet to speak different languages, but a conversation becomes unbalanced if one person teaches for the entire hour. It helps to divide the time and agree on whether corrections should be immediate or saved for later.
Topics should be interesting enough to keep the conversation moving, but not so difficult that one speaker cannot participate.
The goal is not a perfect conversation. It is regular practice in a supportive setting.
question: How can partners make a language exchange balanced?
options: Letting one person speak for the whole hour.
Dividing the time and agreeing how corrections will work.
Choosing topics that nobody understands.
explanation: The first paragraph recommends sharing time and setting expectations about correction.

title: The coast after the tourists leave
author: Tara Singh
dek: A coastal town is more than a summer destination; its residents need a year-round plan.
body: During the holiday season, a seaside town can be full of visitors, restaurants and temporary jobs. In winter, many businesses close and public transport becomes less frequent.
A town that depends on one busy season can struggle to keep young people, maintain services and protect the coast from pressure.
Some communities are developing local food, walking routes and cultural events outside summer so that tourism supports a more stable year.
question: Why do some coastal towns seek activities outside summer?
options: To make the coast less attractive.
To create steadier work, services and income across the year.
To close all restaurants permanently.
explanation: The reading connects year-round activities with stability for residents and services.

title: Practising music without an audience
author: Tariq Moss
dek: Private practice builds the habits that make public performance possible.
body: A musician may spend many sessions repeating a difficult passage that an audience will never notice. The work is slow because the aim is to make a movement reliable before pressure makes it harder.
Practice is not only repetition. It includes listening, changing the tempo and noticing which part of the body becomes tense.
The audience hears the final performance, but the performance depends on hours of quiet attention.
question: What does effective music practice involve?
options: Repeating a passage without listening.
Repetition combined with observation and adjustment.
Performing only in front of an audience.
explanation: The reading says practice includes listening, changing tempo and noticing tension, not repetition alone.

title: The conversation at a farmers' market
author: Priyanka Shah
dek: Farmers' markets make the relationship between food and its producer more visible.
body: At a supermarket, a customer may see a price and a label but rarely meet the person who grew the food. At a farmers' market, the seller can explain when a crop was harvested and how the weather affected it.
This does not make every market automatically fair or affordable. However, conversation can create a better understanding of the work behind a product.
The market is both a place to shop and a place to ask questions.
question: What does a farmers' market make more visible?
options: Only the price of food.
The producer's work and the conditions behind a product.
A guarantee that every item is affordable.
explanation: The reading highlights direct conversation about harvests, weather and labour.

title: The first customer of a small business
author: Felix Moore
dek: For a small business, the first customers provide information as well as income.
body: A new business may begin with an idea that seems clear to its founder. The first customers reveal which parts are useful, confusing or unnecessary.
Listening to that feedback does not mean changing the business after every comment. It means looking for repeated patterns and testing a thoughtful improvement.
Early customers are therefore partners in learning, even when they do not describe themselves that way.
question: How should a new business use early customer feedback?
options: Change everything after one comment.
Look for repeated patterns and test careful improvements.
Ignore customers until the business is large.
explanation: The reading recommends using feedback as evidence, not as an instruction to react to every individual comment.

title: A routine for a calmer morning
author: Grace Ito
dek: A simple morning routine can reduce stress by making the first decisions of the day easier.
body: Some people prepare clothes, breakfast or a short list the night before. This means they do not need to decide everything while they are still waking up.
A routine does not have to be long. Drinking water, opening a window and checking the day's main task may be enough.
The best routine is one that is realistic and easy to repeat.
question: What is one benefit of a simple morning routine?
options: It removes every problem from the day.
It reduces the number of early decisions.
It must take several hours.
explanation: The first paragraph says preparation makes early decisions easier.

title: A Saturday market
author: Amara Cole
dek: A local market can bring food, music and neighbours together in one place.
body: On Saturday morning, stalls sell fruit, bread and handmade objects. A musician plays near the entrance while children walk between the tables.
People come to buy things, but they also stop to talk. Some sellers know their regular customers by name.
The market gives the neighbourhood a busy and friendly meeting place.
question: Why do people visit the market besides shopping?
options: To avoid meeting anyone.
To talk, listen to music and spend time with neighbours.
To buy only imported products.
explanation: The reading describes conversation, music and community as reasons to visit.

title: Reading a weather forecast
author: Owen Price
dek: A forecast helps people plan, but it describes a possibility rather than a promise.
body: A forecast may say that there is a sixty percent chance of rain. This means rain is possible, not that it will rain for exactly sixty percent of the day.
People can use the information to carry an umbrella or change an outdoor plan. They should also check the forecast again because conditions can change.
A forecast is useful when it is read as guidance, not certainty.
question: How should people use a weather forecast?
options: As a guide that can help them plan.
As a promise that cannot change.
By ignoring the chance of rain.
explanation: The final paragraph says a forecast is guidance rather than certainty.

title: The school club after class
author: June Okoro
dek: A school club gives students a chance to learn through shared interests.
body: Every Wednesday, students meet after class to make short films. One student writes a script, another uses the camera and others act or edit.
The group learns technical skills, but it also learns how to listen and work with different ideas.
The final film matters, yet the students also value the teamwork that makes it possible.
question: What do students learn besides technical skills?
options: How to work with different ideas and other people.
How to avoid sharing tasks.
How to finish school early every day.
explanation: The second paragraph mentions listening and teamwork as additional learning.

title: A train station at dawn
author: Lina Haddad
dek: A train station is a place of movement, but it also holds many small moments of waiting.
body: At dawn, passengers check the information board and carry bags towards the platforms. Some people travel for work, while others are beginning a holiday.
The station needs clear signs so that people can find the right train. It also needs seats and shelter for those who arrive early.
Good design helps a busy place feel understandable.
question: What helps a busy station feel understandable?
options: Clear signs and useful places to wait.
Removing all information boards.
Making every platform look different without signs.
explanation: The reading names signs, seats and shelter as useful station features.

title: A better way to learn vocabulary
author: Yuki Sato
dek: New words are easier to remember when learners meet them more than once and use them.
body: A learner can write a new word in a notebook, but writing it once may not be enough. Seeing the word in a sentence and using it in a short message gives the brain more connections.
It is also useful to review words after a day, a week and later in the month. Short, regular practice is often better than one long session.
Vocabulary grows through repeated contact and active use.
question: What can help a learner remember a new word?
options: Writing it once and never using it.
Seeing it in context, using it and reviewing it regularly.
Studying only once a year.
explanation: The text recommends context, active use and spaced review.

title: A morning at the local museum
author: Clara Voss
dek: A local museum can help residents see familiar places through new stories.
body: The museum's new exhibition shows photographs of the town from one hundred years ago. Visitors recognise streets, shops and the river, although the buildings look very different.
A guide explains how people worked, travelled and celebrated at that time. Children ask questions about objects that are no longer common.
The exhibition connects the past of the town with the people who live there today.
question: What does the exhibition help visitors do?
options: See familiar places through their history.
Travel to another country without leaving the museum.
Forget how the town has changed.
explanation: The reading says visitors recognise present-day places and learn about their earlier lives.

title: The useful library card
author: Bea Williams
dek: A library card gives a person access to many books without buying each one.
body: A reader can borrow a novel, return it and choose another book the next week. If the reader does not enjoy a book, the cost of trying it is very small.
Libraries also offer newspapers, computers and sometimes classes. These services help people learn at different ages.
Borrowing makes reading more open because one collection can serve many people.
question: Why is borrowing useful for readers?
options: It allows people to try many books without buying each one.
It means books never need to be returned.
It is only available to children.
explanation: The first paragraph explains that borrowing makes trying different books inexpensive.

title: The lunch that travels well
author: Mina Laurent
dek: A balanced packed lunch is easier to enjoy when it is simple, colourful and prepared safely.
body: A lunch box can include a sandwich, fruit and a small snack. Choosing different colours often means choosing different kinds of food.
Food should be kept cool when necessary, and the box should be cleaned after use. A simple plan makes it easier to prepare lunch the night before.
A good packed lunch gives energy for the rest of the day.
question: What makes a packed lunch easier to prepare?
options: Planning it the night before.
Leaving food in the sun all day.
Using only one kind of food.
explanation: The second paragraph recommends preparing the lunch the night before.

title: The lesson in a first job
author: Marcus Green
dek: A first job can teach practical habits that are useful long after the job ends.
body: A first job may involve serving customers, organising stock or working with a small team. The tasks can seem ordinary, but they teach a person to arrive on time and communicate clearly.
Mistakes are part of the learning process. A helpful manager explains what went wrong and shows the new worker how to improve.
The first job is not only about earning money; it is also a beginning in professional life.
question: What can a first job teach?
options: Only how to spend money.
Habits such as punctuality, communication and learning from mistakes.
That mistakes should always be hidden.
explanation: The reading names punctuality, communication and learning from mistakes as practical lessons.

title: The useful friction of a paper map
author: Elise Warren
dek: A paper map cannot update itself, but its limits can make travellers pay closer attention to where they are.
body: A phone can calculate a route in seconds and quietly correct a wrong turn. A paper map asks more of its reader: the traveller must notice landmarks, estimate distance and connect a small view with a larger landscape.

This effort is inconvenient when a person is late, but it can also produce a stronger sense of place. Instead of moving through a sequence of instructions, the traveller builds a mental picture of the journey.
The value of a paper map is therefore not that it is more accurate than a digital one. Its value lies in the attention required to use it.
question: What is the writer's main argument about paper maps?
options: Their limitations can encourage closer attention to place.
They are always more accurate than digital maps.
They are the best choice whenever someone is late.
explanation: The reading presents the effort of using a paper map as a source of attention, not superior accuracy.

title: A telescope in a school cupboard
author: Jonas Reed
dek: A modest telescope can change science education when students are allowed to use it for their own questions.
body: The telescope in a school cupboard is not powerful enough to produce spectacular images of distant galaxies. It is, however, good enough to show the Moon's changing surface and the movement of bright planets across the sky.

Teachers find that the instrument becomes more valuable when students decide what to observe and keep a record over several weeks. They learn that evidence often grows slowly and that an unexpected result is a reason to look again.
The lesson is larger than astronomy. Students begin to see science as a practice of patient questioning rather than a collection of impressive answers.
question: Why does the telescope become especially valuable to students?
options: It produces the most detailed images available.
It removes the need for teachers to explain science.
It lets them investigate questions and build evidence over time.
explanation: The second paragraph links the telescope's value to student-led observation and patient record-keeping.

title: The neighbourhood that shares tools
author: Mara Okafor
dek: A tool library turns occasional needs into a reason for neighbours to build trust and practical knowledge.
body: Most households need a drill, ladder or pressure washer only occasionally, yet each item takes space and money to own. A tool library lets residents borrow such equipment and return it when a project is finished.

The system depends on more than shelves and membership cards. Borrowers need clear instructions, a way to report damage and a shared expectation that the next person deserves the tool in good condition.

In this arrangement, efficiency and sociability reinforce each other. Sharing reduces unnecessary purchases, while the act of borrowing creates small encounters through which neighbours learn what they can rely on from one another.
question: What does the tool library require in addition to equipment?
options: Clear procedures and a shared responsibility for borrowed items.
A rule that every resident must buy a drill.
A promise that tools will never need repairs.
explanation: The reading says instructions, damage reporting and care for the next borrower are essential to the system.

title: Why accents travel
author: Nadia Bell
dek: An accent can carry the history of movement, contact and belonging without staying fixed in one place.
body: When people move, they do not leave their speech at the border. A new accent may retain sounds associated with home while adopting features heard at school, at work or in the street.

Listeners sometimes treat an accent as a label for a person's character, education or loyalty. That judgement confuses a pattern of pronunciation with a biography, even though the pattern may contain traces of several communities.

Accents are better understood as records of contact. They change because speakers adjust to one another, and those adjustments can express both adaptation and a wish to remain recognisably connected to where they began.
question: What does the writer suggest accents can reveal?
options: A person's character with complete certainty.
That pronunciation never changes after childhood.
The history of contact and movement between communities.
explanation: The reading describes accents as records of movement and interaction, not reliable measures of character.

title: A doorway that welcomes rain
author: Hugo Martins
dek: Small design choices can make a building more comfortable by working with weather instead of sealing it out completely.
body: A covered entrance, a textured floor and a place to leave wet coats may look like minor details. Together they help a building absorb the ordinary arrival of rain without turning every visitor into a problem to be managed.

Designers call such features passive responses because they use shape, materials and airflow before relying on machines. The building still needs heating or cooling, but it asks less of those systems during everyday conditions.
The principle is not romantic resistance to technology. It is a reminder that comfort can be designed into a structure before energy is spent correcting its weaknesses.
question: What principle does the reading illustrate?
options: Good design can use simple physical features to reduce the need for energy.
Buildings should avoid all mechanical systems.
Rain is mainly a problem caused by visitors.
explanation: The reading presents passive features as a way to support comfort before mechanical energy is used.

title: The true cost of convenience
author: Priya Shah
dek: Convenience hides the work and materials that make a disposable service feel effortless.
body: A single-use item is convenient partly because its user does not have to think about cleaning, storage or repair. Those tasks have not disappeared; they have been shifted to factories, waste systems and people whose labour is rarely visible at the moment of purchase.

Reusable alternatives are not automatically better in every situation. They need suitable materials, repeated use and reliable washing or maintenance. The important comparison is therefore not between a perfect option and a bad one, but between the full systems behind different choices.
Once those systems are considered, convenience becomes a design question. We can ask who saves time, who carries the cost and whether a service could be made both practical and less wasteful.
question: What comparison does the writer recommend?
options: The appearance of two products on a shop shelf.
Only the number of times a reusable object is washed.
The complete systems and costs behind different choices.
explanation: The reading says environmental choices should be judged by their full systems, including materials, labour and maintenance.

title: The apprentice question
author: Marcus Green
dek: An apprentice becomes more capable by learning which questions expose the reasoning behind a task.
body: A beginner often asks what to do next. That question is useful, but a more revealing one is why the next step matters and what would count as a warning sign.

Experienced workers may perform a sequence so quickly that its logic becomes invisible. Explaining the choices aloud slows the process enough for both people to notice assumptions, alternatives and points where judgement is required.
The apprentice's aim is not to copy a routine forever. It is to understand the principles well enough to recognise when a familiar routine no longer fits the situation.
question: What kind of understanding should an apprentice develop?
options: An understanding of the reasoning that helps them adapt routines.
The ability to copy every routine without question.
A way to avoid asking experienced workers anything.
explanation: The final paragraph says the apprentice should learn principles and adapt them when circumstances change.

title: A headline's missing verb
author: Daniel Cho
dek: The grammar of a headline can make responsibility visible or leave it strangely out of focus.
body: Compare 'Residents displaced by redevelopment' with 'Company clears homes for redevelopment'. Both may describe the same event, but the first centres on what happened to residents while the second names an actor and an action.

Headlines must be brief, so no wording can include every context. Still, choices about verbs and subjects influence the first question a reader asks: who acted, who was affected and what remains uncertain?
The point is not that one grammatical form is always honest. It is that a careful reader should notice which people and actions a short line places in the foreground.
question: Why does the writer discuss headline grammar?
options: To argue that headlines should be as long as articles.
To prove that passive sentences are always incorrect.
To show that wording can shape how readers see responsibility.
explanation: The examples show that choosing subjects and verbs can foreground either affected people or an actor.

title: The archive of ordinary sounds
author: Clara Voss
dek: Recordings of everyday sounds can preserve a social history that photographs and official documents often miss.
body: An archive of a town may contain speeches, ceremonies and famous performances. A newer collection also records a market closing, a tram bell and the echo of children leaving school.

Such sounds are not neutral souvenirs. The choice of what to record reflects the archivist's attention, and microphones may capture some voices more clearly than others. Even so, ordinary sound can reveal rhythms of work and travel that formal records overlook.
The collection matters because a place is experienced through more than its visible landmarks. Listening to its small noises can help future residents imagine how daily life once felt.
question: What can an archive of ordinary sounds add to historical understanding?
options: Evidence about daily rhythms that formal records may overlook.
A complete record unaffected by selection.
Proof that visual documents are unnecessary.
explanation: The reading values everyday recordings as additional evidence while acknowledging that archives still involve choices.

title: When a satellite looks down
author: Sofia Reed
dek: A satellite image is not a transparent window onto Earth; it becomes useful through careful interpretation.
body: From above, a dry field, a new road and a shallow lake may appear as patterns of colour or texture. Turning those patterns into claims requires comparison with earlier images, local measurements and knowledge of what the sensor can detect.

This is why remote sensing can be powerful without being self-explanatory. An image may show that vegetation has changed, but not by itself establish whether drought, disease or a change in land use caused it.
The strongest analysis treats the image as evidence that prompts questions. It combines a wide view from space with observations made by people closer to the ground.
question: What limitation of satellite images does the writer emphasise?
options: They cannot show any changes on Earth.
They are less useful than photographs taken indoors.
They require other evidence and local knowledge to explain what they show.
explanation: The reading distinguishes between detecting a pattern and explaining its cause, which requires additional evidence.

title: The value of a public rehearsal
author: Mina Laurent
dek: A rehearsal held in public can turn an audience from a final judge into a participant in a work's development.
body: When a theatre company rehearses in a square, passers-by see pauses, repeated scenes and occasional mistakes. The work is not presented as finished, so people can respond to its questions rather than merely approve or reject a polished result.
Artists do not accept every suggestion. They listen for reactions that reveal confusion, curiosity or an unexpected emotional connection, then decide which observations belong to the piece.
Public rehearsal therefore creates a particular form of exchange. It makes artistic process visible while allowing the makers to test how a work meets people outside the rehearsal room.
question: How does a public rehearsal change the audience's role?
options: It lets the audience respond during the work's development.
It makes the audience responsible for writing the entire work.
It guarantees that every suggestion will be accepted.
explanation: The reading says passers-by can react to an unfinished work and contribute observations without controlling the final decisions.

title: An island of cool roofs
author: Elena Ruiz
dek: Changing the surface of a roof can reduce heat indoors, but the benefits depend on who can make the change.
body: A light-coloured roof reflects more sunlight than a dark one and may keep a building cooler during hot weather. In a dense neighbourhood, many such roofs can also reduce the amount of heat held by the built environment.
The measure is relatively simple, yet it is not equally available to everyone. Tenants may not control a roof, and households with limited money may have more urgent repairs to fund.

A cool-roof programme is most effective when it treats access as part of the design. Public support, clear information and attention to rented homes can turn a technical idea into a fairer form of heat protection.
question: What condition does the writer add to the success of cool roofs?
options: They must be installed only on new buildings.
They work only when every roof is dark.
People who need the protection must be able to access it.
explanation: The reading argues that a technical measure helps fairly only when tenants and lower-income households can access it.

title: The queue as a social contract
author: Amara Cole
dek: A queue is a small agreement to accept an order that protects strangers from having to negotiate every turn.
body: Standing in line can feel inefficient, especially when the next available service is uncertain. Yet the shared order reduces a different kind of effort: people do not need to argue about who arrived first or watch every neighbour for signs of unfairness.

Queues are not automatically fair. A person may need priority because of disability, and a badly designed service may force people to wait for hours. The social rule works only when its purpose is understood and exceptions are visible rather than arbitrary.
Seen this way, queuing is a modest form of cooperation. It asks individuals to surrender a little immediate advantage in exchange for a predictable process.
question: Why can a queue be understood as cooperation?
options: It replaces repeated negotiations with a predictable shared order.
It guarantees that nobody will ever wait for long.
It prevents services from making any exceptions.
explanation: The reading describes a queue as an agreement that reduces conflict by creating a predictable order, while allowing justified exceptions.

title: The office with no centre
author: Ruth Okafor
dek: A distributed team can work well when it replaces the office's informal signals with deliberate ways of sharing context.
body: In a shared office, a colleague's expression or a conversation near the kitchen may reveal that a deadline has moved. Remote colleagues do not receive these signals by accident, so important context must travel through clearer channels.

That does not mean filling every hour with meetings. A short written decision log, predictable times for questions and an explicit record of ownership can give people the information they need without constant interruption.

The challenge is cultural as much as technical. A team becomes genuinely distributed when access to context depends on reliable practices rather than on being physically near the loudest conversation.
question: What does a distributed team need to replace informal office signals?
options: More meetings in every part of the day.
A rule that only remote workers communicate in writing.
Deliberate practices for recording decisions, questions and ownership.
explanation: The reading recommends lightweight, reliable practices that make context available without creating constant meetings.

title: What a translation leaves open
author: Nora Ellis
dek: A good translation carries meaning across languages while admitting that some associations cannot be transferred exactly.
body: A translator can preserve the basic event in a sentence, yet a word may also carry a rhythm, social register or echo of another text. Replacing it with a direct dictionary equivalent may keep the information while losing part of its atmosphere.

Translators respond by making choices. They may recreate an effect with different words, leave a small strangeness in place or add a note that invites the reader to notice what does not travel easily.
The result is not a failed copy. It is a new route between texts, one that makes the original available while showing that languages organise experience in different ways.
question: How does the writer view the parts of meaning that do not transfer exactly?
options: As features that translators must handle through conscious choices.
As proof that translation is impossible.
As details that dictionaries always preserve.
explanation: The reading presents translation as a series of choices about rhythm, register and cultural associations.

title: The ethics of a perfect photograph
author: Ibrahim Wells
dek: A technically perfect image can still be ethically careless if it removes the people and circumstances that give an event meaning.
body: Sharp focus, balanced light and a dramatic angle can make a photograph immediately persuasive. They do not, however, tell viewers whether the subject agreed to be shown or whether the framing turns a person's difficulty into an attractive spectacle.
Photographers must make choices before and after the shutter clicks. They can explain the context, ask how an image will be used and resist edits that make a complicated situation look simple.
The question is not whether photographs should be beautiful. It is whether beauty is serving understanding or asking viewers to admire an image while overlooking the person inside it.
question: What ethical question should accompany technical quality?
options: Whether the image uses the most expensive camera.
Whether every photograph should be unattractive.
Whether the image respects its subject and supports understanding.
explanation: The reading separates technical quality from ethical care for the subject and the surrounding context.

title: A street designed for mistakes
author: Jon Bell
dek: A forgiving street lets people recover from a wrong turn without turning a small mistake into danger or embarrassment.
body: A clear crossing, a bench near a busy stop and signs visible before a junction all help people who are distracted, unfamiliar with the area or moving at a different pace. They create time to notice and correct an error.

Designers sometimes imagine the ideal user as someone who knows the rules and pays attention at every moment. Real streets contain children, visitors, older people and tired commuters, so a safe design must expect variation rather than blame it.
A forgiving street is not an invitation to ignore responsibility. It is an acknowledgement that public space works better when ordinary mistakes have ordinary consequences instead of severe ones.
question: What does a forgiving street do?
options: It gives people ways to recover from ordinary mistakes safely.
It removes the need for people to pay attention.
It is designed only for visitors unfamiliar with the city.
explanation: The reading defines forgiving design as providing time and space to correct errors without severe consequences.

title: The second measure of a forest
author: Tara Singh
dek: Counting trees matters, but a forest's condition also depends on relationships among species, soil and water.
body: A plantation can contain thousands of trees and still offer little variety of habitat. A more complex forest may look less uniform while supporting fungi, insects, birds and plants that depend on one another.
This does not make a simple tree count useless. Numbers help track planting and loss, but they answer a narrower question than one about ecological health.
The second measure is therefore not a single replacement number. It is a richer account of diversity, connections and the ability of the forest to recover after disturbance.
question: Why is a tree count an incomplete measure of forest health?
options: Trees cannot be counted reliably in any forest.
A complex forest always has fewer trees than a plantation.
It records quantity but not the wider relationships that support habitat and recovery.
explanation: The reading accepts counting as useful while explaining that it does not capture diversity, ecological relationships or resilience.

title: A machine that explains itself
author: Amir Patel
dek: When an automated system affects people, an explanation should help them question and respond to its decision.
body: A message saying that a system has rejected an application may be called an explanation, but it gives little help if the person cannot tell which information mattered or how to correct an error.

Useful explanations do not need to reveal every line of code. They should identify the factors that influenced a result, describe relevant uncertainty and provide a route for review when the decision has serious consequences.
Explainability is therefore not a decorative feature added after a system is built. It is part of making the system accountable to the people who must live with its outputs.
question: What makes an automated explanation useful?
options: It shows influential factors, uncertainty and a way to request review.
It reveals every line of code to every user.
It makes all automated decisions final.
explanation: The reading defines useful explanation through understandable factors, uncertainty and access to review.

title: The museum label as a conversation
author: Bea Williams
dek: A museum label does more than identify an object; it invites visitors to decide how they will look at it.
body: A label can give a date and a material, but it can also mention uncertainty, a missing voice or a question that remains unresolved. Such information changes the visitor's role from receiving a finished fact to considering how knowledge was assembled.

Too much text can overwhelm a gallery, while too little can make an object appear self-explanatory. The strongest labels choose a clear point of entry and leave enough space for the object and the visitor's own attention.
A label becomes conversational when it offers orientation without closing interpretation. It says, in effect, 'Here is something to notice; now look again.'
question: When does a museum label become conversational?
options: When it gives every possible interpretation.
When it contains only the object's date.
When it guides attention without pretending to close interpretation.
explanation: The final paragraph describes a conversational label as an invitation to notice, not a complete replacement for looking.

title: The confidence of a small experiment
author: Felix Moore
dek: A small, reversible experiment can replace a confident argument about an uncertain idea with evidence from practice.
body: Teams often debate whether a new process will save time, improve quality or frustrate customers. The debate may be useful, but it cannot reveal how the process behaves in the real setting where interruptions and exceptions occur.
A limited trial creates a safer way to learn. The team can define what it will observe, run the change with a small group and stop or adjust it if the results are poor.
The experiment does not remove judgement. It makes judgement better informed by turning a prediction into something that can be observed and discussed.
question: What is the main advantage of a small experiment?
options: It produces practical evidence while keeping the consequences reversible.
It guarantees that a new idea will succeed.
It makes discussion and judgement unnecessary.
explanation: The reading values small trials because they test predictions in practice without committing the whole team to an irreversible change.

title: A neighbourhood calendar
author: June Okoro
dek: A shared calendar can make a neighbourhood's care work visible and prevent useful events from competing by accident.
body: A food collection, a parents' meeting and a repair afternoon may be organised by different groups. Without a common calendar, each group can work hard while making it difficult for the same volunteers to attend more than one activity.

The calendar is not merely an online list. Someone must maintain it, check that information is accessible and make room for events that are informal or organised by people with limited digital access.

Its deeper value is coordination. By showing when and where work happens, the calendar helps residents see that community life is built from many small contributions rather than from one central organisation.
question: What is the deeper value of the shared calendar?
options: It replaces all neighbourhood organisations.
It ensures that every resident attends every event.
It makes separate contributions visible and easier to coordinate.
explanation: The reading presents the calendar as a coordination tool that reveals the many contributors to community life.

title: The grammar of a warning
author: Nora Ellis
dek: A public warning must be clear about both the danger and the action people can take.
body: 'Be careful' sounds urgent but leaves a reader asking what to avoid, how serious the risk is and where to find help. A more useful warning names the situation, gives a practical instruction and states when the advice applies.
Precision does not require cold language. People are more likely to act when a message respects their ability to understand the reason for a request instead of using fear as its only tool.
The grammar of a warning is therefore part of its public responsibility. Clarity helps people make a decision; drama alone may only make them anxious.
question: What should a useful public warning provide?
options: A clear description of the risk and a practical action.
Urgency without any explanation.
A dramatic image instead of instructions.
explanation: The first paragraph lists the risk, the relevant conditions and an actionable instruction as elements of a useful warning.

title: A roof with a second job
author: Elena Ruiz
dek: A roof can protect a building while also storing rain, producing energy or supporting life above the street.
body: Roofs are often treated as the final surface of a building, seen from below and forgotten. Yet a roof receives sunlight and rainfall directly, making it a useful place for functions that would otherwise require extra land.

A planted roof can slow the movement of rain into drains, while panels can turn sunlight into electricity. Neither solution is universal: weight, maintenance, shade and ownership all affect what is possible.
The broader design lesson is to ask what each surface is already receiving and whether it can perform more than one useful role. A second job is valuable when it fits the building's real conditions.
question: What is the broader design lesson in the reading?
options: Every roof should use the same technology.
Roofs are valuable only because they can be seen from above.
A building surface may perform several useful roles when local conditions allow it.
explanation: The final paragraph recommends designing multiple functions around a surface's actual conditions rather than applying one universal solution.

title: The memory stored in a seed
author: Maya Chen
dek: Saving diverse seeds preserves options for farms and gardens facing conditions that cannot yet be predicted.
body: A seed bank may look like a quiet collection of small packets. Each packet can represent a set of traits: tolerance of dry soil, a different growing season or resistance to a local problem.

Storing seeds is not enough. They must be labelled accurately, checked for viability and sometimes grown again so that knowledge about their use does not disappear. Farmers' experience is part of the collection too.
Seed saving is therefore a form of preparation rather than nostalgia. Diversity keeps more possible responses available when climate, pests or diets change.
question: Why does the reading say seed saving is a form of preparation?
options: Because diversity preserves possible responses to future conditions.
Because every saved seed will certainly be needed.
Because seeds can be stored without labels or care.
explanation: The reading connects diversity with keeping options open for uncertain changes in climate, pests and food needs.

title: The garden that teaches by failure
author: Mina Laurent
dek: A community garden becomes a classroom when failed crops are treated as useful information rather than personal embarrassment.
body: The first season produces more questions than vegetables. Some seedlings dry out, while others grow leaves but no fruit. The gardeners compare notes instead of quietly replacing the evidence of what went wrong.
They learn that the same advice does not fit every plot. Soil, shade and watering habits change the result, so the group tests small adjustments rather than searching for one perfect rule.
The garden's harvest matters, but so does its method. People become more confident because mistakes are discussed openly and turned into better observations.
question: What makes the garden a useful classroom?
options: Every crop grows successfully on the first attempt.
The group follows one rule without adapting it.
Gardeners use failures to compare conditions and improve their observations.
explanation: The reading says failed crops become useful when gardeners discuss them and test adjustments.

title: A map for lost luggage
author: Owen Price
dek: A simple tracking label can make a frustrating problem easier to investigate, provided its information is kept current.
body: A traveller attaches a small label with a contact number and a code to a suitcase. If the bag is found, staff can identify its owner without opening it or relying on a hurried description.
The label is helpful, not magical. The contact details must be readable, the traveller must report a change of address and the system must protect information that does not need to be public.
Good tracking tools reduce uncertainty by giving people a shared reference. They work best when their small practical requirements are treated as part of the design.
question: What condition is necessary for the tracking label to work well?
options: Its information must be accurate, readable and appropriately protected.
It must reveal every detail about the traveller.
It must replace the airline's responsibility for luggage.
explanation: The second paragraph identifies current, readable and protected information as necessary conditions.

title: The neighbours who share childcare
author: Amara Cole
dek: Informal childcare networks can help families, but they become sustainable when generosity is balanced with clear limits.
body: One neighbour collects two children from school on Tuesdays; another helps when a parent has an evening shift. The arrangement begins with goodwill, yet goodwill alone can make people unsure about what they have promised.

The group therefore agrees on basic details: how much notice is needed, which adults may collect a child and what happens if someone is ill. These rules do not make the network less caring; they make it easier to trust.
Shared care is not a replacement for every formal service. It is a local form of support that works when its participants can say both 'I can help' and 'I cannot do that today.'
question: Why does the childcare network need clear limits?
options: To stop neighbours from helping one another.
To ensure that no formal childcare service is ever used.
To make responsibilities understandable and trust more durable.
explanation: The agreed details clarify responsibilities, which allows the informal support to continue reliably.

title: Why some words sound old
author: Yuki Sato
dek: Words can sound old when the situations and groups that once used them become less familiar.
body: A word may remain in dictionaries long after it becomes rare in conversation. Younger speakers then meet it mainly in books, ceremonies or historical dramas, where it acquires an old-fashioned atmosphere.
The word has not necessarily changed its meaning. What has changed is the company it keeps and the settings in which people hear it.
This is why vocabulary carries social time as well as definition. A listener recognises not only what a word refers to, but also the period or style it seems to belong to.
question: What can make a word sound old-fashioned?
options: It is heard mainly in older or formal settings that are less familiar today.
Its meaning must always have disappeared.
Dictionaries remove it from the language immediately.
explanation: The reading links an old-fashioned feeling to the settings and speakers associated with a word, not necessarily to a lost meaning.

title: The bench beside the lift
author: Lina Haddad
dek: A small seat near a lift can make a public building easier to use for people whose journeys take more time.
body: In a busy station, people often stand near the lift while waiting for a companion, recovering from a steep route or deciding where to go next. Without a seat, they may have to block the entrance or leave the area.
The bench is not a dramatic architectural feature. Its usefulness comes from anticipating ordinary pauses and keeping them separate from the moving stream of passengers.
Accessible design often works in this quiet way. It adds choices for people who need them without making the whole space harder for everyone else to navigate.
question: What problem does the bench help solve?
options: It makes lifts travel faster.
It removes the need for signs in the station.
It provides a place for necessary pauses without blocking movement.
explanation: The reading explains that the bench accommodates waiting and recovery while keeping the entrance clear.

title: The second use of a jar
author: Bea Williams
dek: Reusing a container can be useful, but its success depends on whether the new use is realistic enough to last.
body: A glass jar can hold spices, screws or a packed lunch after its original contents are gone. The environmental benefit comes from extending the object's useful life rather than buying another container immediately.
However, reuse is not a moral test. A jar that is difficult to clean, too heavy to carry or unsuitable for a particular food may sit unused and eventually become waste.
The sensible question is not whether every object can be reused forever. It is whether a new use fits people's routines well enough to prevent an unnecessary replacement.
question: What makes reusing a jar environmentally useful?
options: The new use must be practical enough to extend its life.
The jar must be used for every possible purpose.
A reused jar never needs cleaning.
explanation: The reading says reuse helps when it is realistic and keeps the object in service instead of leading to an unnecessary replacement.

title: A checklist for creative work
author: Leah Morgan
dek: A checklist can support creativity when it protects important steps without dictating every decision.
body: Artists and designers sometimes avoid checklists because they associate them with routine work. Yet remembering to save a version, test an idea with a reader or check a permission can protect the time spent on invention.
The list should cover reliable actions, not prescribe the shape of the final idea. If it becomes too detailed, it may turn a flexible process into a search for compliance.
Used well, a checklist clears mental space. It handles small preventable risks so that creative attention can remain on the choices that genuinely require imagination.
question: How should a checklist support creative work?
options: By deciding every artistic choice in advance.
By replacing the need to test ideas.
By protecting reliable steps while leaving creative decisions open.
explanation: The reading recommends using checklists for repeatable safeguards, not for controlling the creative outcome.

title: The photograph with a caption
author: Clara Voss
dek: A caption does not merely describe a photograph; it gives viewers information that can change what they think they are seeing.
body: A picture of an empty street might suggest abandonment until a caption explains that it was taken before a parade. A portrait may look private until the caption identifies a public ceremony and the person's role in it.

Context cannot fix a misleading image, but without it viewers may fill gaps with assumptions. Editors therefore need to check dates, places and names instead of treating a caption as decoration added at the end.
A responsible caption is short, but its work is substantial: it helps an image become evidence rather than an invitation to guess.
question: Why is a caption important in the reading?
options: It supplies context that can prevent viewers from filling gaps with assumptions.
It makes every photograph objective.
It replaces the need to check the photograph's source.
explanation: The reading says accurate captions provide context and reduce misleading assumptions, although they cannot make an image objective by themselves.

title: The small planetarium
author: Sofia Reed
dek: A small planetarium can make distant science feel local by connecting a large subject with a familiar room.
body: The dome is modest, and its projector cannot show every object in the sky. What it can do is place visitors under a clear, moving map while a guide explains how to find a few stars from the town outside.
Children often remember the moment they recognise the same pattern later in the real sky. The experience turns an abstract diagram into a question they can test for themselves.
The planetarium's power comes less from scale than from connection. It gives people a starting point for looking beyond the room.
question: What is the planetarium's main educational value?
options: It shows every object in space with complete detail.
It means visitors never need to look at the real sky.
It connects an abstract map with observations people can make themselves.
explanation: The reading emphasises the link between the dome's map and later observation outdoors.

title: An evening without streetlights
author: Elena Ruiz
dek: Reducing unnecessary light at night can help wildlife and the night sky, but public safety still requires careful planning.
body: A park does not need every path to be as bright as a main road. Lower lights, timers and darker areas can reduce glare and allow insects and birds to follow more natural patterns.
People also need to feel able to move safely. Designers can place light where it helps people see crossings and faces, while avoiding a blanket of brightness that reaches every tree and window.
The goal is not a completely dark city. It is a more thoughtful night-time environment in which visibility, rest and ecological needs are considered together.
question: What balance does the reading propose?
options: Keeping every public space completely dark.
Using light where it supports safety while reducing unnecessary brightness.
Making every path as bright as a main road.
explanation: The reading supports targeted lighting that preserves safety while limiting excess light.

title: The archive box
author: Nora Ellis
dek: An archive box becomes meaningful when its contents are labelled well enough for someone else to find a story later.
body: A family places letters, tickets and photographs in one box, intending to organise it someday. Years later, the objects are still together, but nobody knows which journey a ticket belongs to or who appears in a faded picture.
A simple note with names, places and approximate dates can change the collection's future. It does not need to explain everything; it only needs to preserve clues that memory may no longer supply.
Archiving is therefore an act of generosity towards an unknown reader. Organisation turns private fragments into material that another person can understand and question.
question: What gives the archive box greater future value?
options: Keeping all objects anonymous.
Throwing away objects that do not tell a complete story.
Adding basic information that helps another person identify them.
explanation: The reading says names, places and dates preserve clues that future readers may need.

title: When a recipe travels
author: Mina Laurent
dek: A recipe changes as it travels because cooks adapt it to local ingredients, equipment and expectations.
body: A dish may cross a border in a notebook or in someone's memory. The next cook changes the flour, replaces a spice or adjusts the cooking time because the original ingredient is unavailable.
Some people see these changes as a loss of authenticity. Others see them as evidence that a recipe remains alive rather than frozen at one historical moment.
Both views identify something real: a dish has a history, but its history includes the choices people make when they carry it into a new kitchen.
question: What does the reading suggest about a travelling recipe?
options: It must remain exactly unchanged to have a history.
Its adaptations become part of the history of how people carry it.
Local ingredients never affect cooking traditions.
explanation: The final paragraph treats adaptation as part of a recipe's continuing history, not as proof that it has no past.

title: A question before a statistic
author: Daniel Cho
dek: A statistic becomes more informative when readers know what was counted, compared and left outside the number.
body: The sentence 'half of residents support the plan' sounds precise. Before accepting it, a careful reader asks how many residents were surveyed, how the question was worded and whether the people asked represent the wider population.
These questions do not make statistics useless. They show that a number is the result of a method, not a fact that arrived without choices.
Reading numbers well means being interested in both the result and the process that produced it.
question: What should a reader ask before accepting a statistic?
options: Whether the number looks impressive.
Whether all statistics should be ignored.
What was counted, how it was measured and whom it represents.
explanation: The reading recommends examining the sample, wording and representativeness behind a number.

title: The repair lesson
author: Felix Moore
dek: A repair teaches more than the answer to one fault when the person doing it learns how to diagnose the next one.
body: A technician could replace a damaged part quickly and send the device back. A more useful lesson shows the customer how to notice the signs that led to the diagnosis and which simple checks are safe to perform.
This takes a little longer, and not every repair can become a full course. Even a brief explanation can change a person from a passive owner into someone who understands the limits of the object.
Knowledge does not prevent every breakdown. It makes future decisions less mysterious and can help people repair, maintain or replace things more thoughtfully.
question: What makes a repair educational?
options: It guarantees that the device will never fail again.
It explains the diagnosis so the person can make better future decisions.
It avoids telling the owner anything about the fault.
explanation: The reading values explanations that build diagnostic understanding, even though they cannot prevent every future failure.

title: A pond in a schoolyard
author: Tara Singh
dek: A small pond can become both habitat and a lesson in how living systems change over time.
body: At first, the pond seems empty except for rainwater. Weeks later, plants appear at its edge and insects visit the surface. Students record these changes instead of expecting the space to look the same every day.
The pond also needs care. Litter must be removed, paths kept safe and the water observed for signs of trouble. A wild-looking place is not a place without responsibility.
Its educational value lies in making change visible. Students see that habitats are active relationships, not static decorations placed in a garden.
question: What do students learn from observing the pond?
options: That a habitat should never change.
That a wild-looking space requires no maintenance.
That living systems develop over time and need care.
explanation: The reading combines observation of ecological change with the responsibility needed to maintain a safe habitat.

title: The museum volunteer
author: Clara Voss
dek: A museum volunteer can make a collection feel local by connecting objects with the memories and questions of visitors.
body: The volunteer does not need to know every date in the catalogue. Their role is often to welcome people, listen to what an object reminds them of and pass useful questions to the museum team.
A visitor may recognise a tool that once belonged in a family workshop, while a child may ask why an object was made from a material no longer common. These responses add routes into the collection.
Volunteering is therefore not simply free staffing. When it is supported and respected, it helps a museum hear the community it hopes to serve.
question: What is the volunteer's distinctive contribution?
options: Memorising every catalogue entry without speaking to visitors.
Connecting visitors' experiences and questions with the museum's work.
Replacing the museum team in all professional decisions.
explanation: The reading presents the volunteer as a bridge between visitors' responses and the museum team.

title: A train with no announcement
author: Lina Haddad
dek: A quiet train journey is more accessible when information is available in more than one form.
body: A passenger may not hear an announcement because of noise, hearing loss or an unfamiliar language. Clear screens, visible route maps and notices at the platform provide other ways to understand what is happening.
Different forms of information also help when a service changes suddenly. A screen can show a new platform while staff point a traveller towards it, combining written, spoken and human guidance.
Accessibility is not achieved by choosing one perfect channel. It comes from giving people several reliable ways to reach the same essential information.
question: What does the reading mean by several reliable channels?
options: Repeating the same announcement more loudly.
Removing staff because screens can answer every question.
Providing essential information through visual, spoken and human forms.
explanation: The text gives screens, route maps, spoken notices and staff guidance as complementary ways to convey information.

title: What a password manager changes
author: Amir Patel
dek: A password manager can make safer digital habits easier by reducing the amount a person has to remember.
body: People often reuse a familiar password because remembering a different one for every service is difficult. A password manager stores complex passwords and fills them when needed, while the user protects the manager with one strong main password.
The tool is not a substitute for care. Its account needs protection, devices should be kept secure and important settings still deserve attention.
Its main benefit is behavioural: by lowering the memory burden, it makes a safer choice more convenient than repeating the same password.
question: What behavioural change does a password manager encourage?
options: Using one familiar password everywhere.
Using different, stronger passwords without having to remember each one.
Ignoring the security of the manager's main account.
explanation: The reading says the manager reduces memory demands, making distinct stronger passwords easier to use.

title: The sound of a new word
author: Nadia Bell
dek: When a new word enters a language, speakers often test its sound and shape before its form settles.
body: People may pronounce a borrowed word in several ways, spell it differently in messages or add a familiar ending. These variations are not necessarily confusion; they are signs that speakers are fitting an unfamiliar item into a known system.
Over time, schools, media and ordinary conversation may favour one form. Another may remain in a particular region or group, carrying a different sense of belonging.
A new word's journey shows that language is built through use. Rules describe the result, but speakers create the pattern by trying it out together.
question: What do early variations in a borrowed word show?
options: That speakers are fitting it into their existing language system.
That the word has no meaning in any community.
That one pronunciation is always chosen immediately.
explanation: The first paragraph presents variation as the process of adapting a borrowed word to a familiar system.

title: A fairer timetable
author: Ruth Okafor
dek: A timetable becomes fairer when it recognises different kinds of time instead of treating every hour as equally available.
body: A meeting scheduled at the end of the day may be easy for one worker and impossible for another who collects a child or travels a long distance. Repeating the same time protects convenience for some people while quietly excluding others.
A team can rotate meeting times, record decisions and decide which conversations truly need everyone present. These changes do not remove all inconvenience, but they distribute it more evenly.
Fair scheduling is therefore a practical form of inclusion. It asks who is being assumed when a calendar is designed.
question: How can a team make its timetable fairer?
options: Keep every meeting at the same convenient time for one group.
Stop recording decisions so nobody misses a meeting.
Rotate times and reduce unnecessary demands for simultaneous attendance.
explanation: The reading recommends rotating times and using records so the inconvenience and access are shared more fairly.

title: The door left open
author: June Okoro
dek: A community centre's open door matters when people can enter without already knowing what they are allowed to ask for.
body: The centre offers advice, classes and a warm place to sit. A sign lists the services, but a person who feels uncertain may still hesitate at the entrance.

Staff therefore greet visitors without demanding a reason immediately. They explain what is available, help people find the right room and accept that some visitors need time before they can describe a problem.
Being welcoming is not only a friendly mood. It is a set of small practices that lowers the cost of asking for help.
question: What makes the community centre genuinely welcoming?
options: A sign that lists services without any human contact.
Practices that help uncertain visitors enter, understand options and ask for help.
A rule that visitors must explain their problem before entering.
explanation: The reading defines welcome through practical support that makes entering and asking for help easier.

title: A product that ages well
author: Hugo Martins
dek: A well-designed product can become more valuable over time when it is repairable, adaptable and allowed to show use.
body: Some objects are judged as failures when they acquire scratches or need a small repair. Others are made with parts that can be replaced and surfaces that become familiar rather than simply worn out.
Ageing well does not mean pretending that damage is beautiful or that every old object should be kept. It means giving an object a reasonable chance to remain useful as circumstances change.
Designing for a longer life changes the relationship between owner and product. Care becomes part of use, not evidence that the object has already been discarded in spirit.
question: What does it mean for a product to age well?
options: It must never show any signs of use.
It can remain useful through repair, adaptation and ordinary wear.
It should be kept even when it no longer serves a purpose.
explanation: The reading links ageing well to continued usefulness, repair and adaptation rather than perfect appearance or endless keeping.

title: The weather diary
author: Owen Price
