dek: A personal weather diary cannot replace official measurements, but it can help someone notice patterns in a familiar place.
body: A gardener writes down the first frost, the days when the soil is dry and the dates when flowers open. One observation proves little, but years of notes can change the gardener's sense of what is early, late or unusual.
Memory is unreliable, so the diary is most useful when it follows the same simple method each year. The notes can then be compared with forecasts or local records.
Keeping the diary turns weather from background scenery into something observed. It encourages attention without claiming to be a complete scientific record.
question: What is the weather diary useful for?
options: Replacing all official weather measurements.
Helping a person notice and compare local patterns over time.
Proving that one unusual day changes the climate.
explanation: The reading presents consistent personal notes as a way to notice local patterns, while clearly limiting what they can prove.

title: The pause before sharing
author: Ibrahim Wells
dek: A short pause before sharing a post can interrupt the feeling that attention must become action immediately.
body: A surprising headline creates a small pressure to pass it on before anyone else. The reader may know that the source is unfamiliar, yet the speed of the feed makes checking feel like a delay rather than part of responsible use.
A useful pause asks what the claim says, where it came from and what would happen if it were wrong. Sometimes the answer is to share it with a correction; sometimes it is to close the page.
The pause is not scepticism for its own sake. It is a way to give judgement a moment to catch up with emotion.
question: What is the purpose of the pause before sharing?
options: To prevent anyone from expressing an opinion online.
To give judgement time to check a claim and its possible consequences.
To make every post take as long as a full investigation.
explanation: The reading recommends a brief check of source, claim and consequences before deciding whether to share.

title: The value of a familiar route
author: Jon Bell
dek: A familiar route can support confidence and independence, especially when its signs and landmarks remain consistent.
body: A person learning a new journey may rely on the bakery at the corner, the colour of a bridge or the sound of a crossing signal. These details turn a sequence of streets into a route that can be remembered.

If signs move or a pavement closes without clear information, the route becomes uncertain even when the distance has not changed. Consistency is a form of assistance that does not require another person to guide every step.
Good wayfinding is therefore more than pointing towards a destination. It helps people build a mental map they can use again.
question: Why are consistent signs and landmarks valuable?
options: They make every journey shorter.
They help people build a route they can remember and use independently.
They remove the need to explain temporary changes.
explanation: The reading says consistent cues turn streets into a memorable route and support independent travel.

title: The first question at a clinic
author: Grace Ito
dek: A respectful first question can help a service understand what a visitor needs before offering a ready-made solution.
body: At a busy clinic, a receptionist could point every visitor towards the same form. Asking what brings someone in may reveal that they need an interpreter, a quieter place or help finding the right department.
The question does not solve the problem by itself. It creates a moment in which the person can describe their situation instead of being forced to choose from categories they may not understand.
Good service begins with attention. Before improving a process, it is often worth finding out what the person in front of you is actually trying to do.
question: What does the first question make possible?
options: It guarantees that every problem is solved immediately.
It helps staff understand a person's need before directing them.
It removes the need for forms or departments.
explanation: The reading says the question reveals needs that a standard form or direction might miss.

title: A switch for the kettle
author: Amir Patel
dek: A small change in the way a device is switched off can make an energy-saving habit easier to repeat.
body: A kettle may stop heating when its button is pressed, yet another part of the setup can continue drawing a little power. A switch that clearly disconnects the appliance makes the final action visible.
The saving from one kettle is modest. The design matters because it removes uncertainty: people can tell whether the device is still connected and can repeat the action without checking a manual.
Technology is often discussed as a source of new capability. It can also be valuable when it makes a sensible everyday habit simpler.
question: Why does the switch help people form a habit?
options: It makes the kettle boil faster in every situation.
It makes the final action clear and easy to repeat.
It removes the need for electricity.
explanation: The reading connects the switch with visible, repeatable action rather than with faster boiling or eliminating electricity.

title: The letter in two alphabets
author: Nadia Bell
dek: A family letter written in two alphabets can show how people move between identities without choosing only one.
body: One page begins in the script used at home and continues in the alphabet used at school. The writer changes not because one system is more real, but because each makes a different audience or memory feel close.
A reader who knows only one alphabet may see the switches as inconsistency. For the writer, they are part of an ordinary linguistic life shaped by education, relatives and place.
The letter is therefore more than a message. Its visual form records the communities that the writer could address and the worlds they carried together.
question: What do the changes of alphabet reveal?
options: That the writer could not decide what language to use.
That the writer moved between different communities and audiences.
That one alphabet was always considered superior.
explanation: The reading interprets the switches as evidence of different audiences and parts of the writer's linguistic life.

title: A window at eye level
author: Hugo Martins
dek: A window is useful not only because it brings in light, but because its height can connect a room with the life outside.
body: A high window may brighten a room while showing only sky. A lower opening can let a seated person see a garden, a pavement or another face, creating a visual connection without requiring anyone to stand up.
Privacy still matters, especially on a busy street. Curtains, planting and careful angles can allow light and outlook without making every interior visible.
Good window design balances several needs. It treats a view as part of comfort while recognising that comfort includes control over what can be seen.
question: What does the reading say window height can affect?
options: Only the price of the glass.
Whether people can connect with the outside while remaining comfortable and private.
Whether a room needs any walls.
explanation: The reading links window height with outlook, comfort and privacy, not merely with cost or the existence of walls.

title: The lunchbox audit
author: Priya Shah
dek: Looking at what returns uneaten can help a household reduce food waste without turning meals into a test of virtue.
body: For a week, a family notes which foods come home from school or work. They discover that the problem is not simply that someone dislikes vegetables; the fruit is often too difficult to eat quickly and the portions are too large.

The family changes one thing at a time: smaller servings, easier packaging and a choice about what to save safely for later. They keep the foods that people enjoy instead of treating waste reduction as punishment.
The audit works because it follows actual habits. It turns a vague feeling of waste into information that can guide a practical change.
question: Why is the lunchbox audit useful?
options: It proves that every family should eat the same foods.
It identifies specific habits that can be changed realistically.
It makes enjoying food less important than avoiding all leftovers.
explanation: The reading shows that observing actual waste reveals practical changes in portions, packaging and storage.

title: The work of starting
author: Leah Morgan
dek: Beginning a difficult task often becomes easier when the first action is made small enough to be unthreatening.
body: A report can feel impossible when it is imagined as a finished document. Opening a blank page and writing three questions changes the task from producing an answer to preparing a place for thinking.
The first step is not meant to be impressive. Its purpose is to create movement and information. Once a draft, list or sketch exists, the next decision is usually easier to see.
Starting well is therefore a design problem. A person can reduce resistance by choosing an action that is concrete, limited and connected to the larger task.
question: What is the purpose of a small first action?
options: To finish the whole task immediately.
To create movement and information so the next decision is easier.
To avoid deciding what the larger task involves.
explanation: The reading says a small beginning turns an abstract task into something visible and makes the next choice clearer.

title: When a photo goes viral
author: Daniel Cho
dek: The speed of a viral photograph can separate an image from the information needed to understand it fairly.
body: A photograph spreads because it seems to say everything at a glance. People add explanations in comments, and soon a location, date or identity may be repeated as if it had been checked.

The image may still be important, but its popularity is not evidence that the accompanying story is true. Editors and users need to trace the original source and distinguish what is visible from what is inferred.
Sharing responsibly does not require waiting for perfect knowledge. It requires being honest about what the photograph shows and what remains uncertain.
question: What should people distinguish when sharing a viral photograph?
options: The image's popularity from the facts that have been verified.
The size of the file from the colour of the image.
A photograph from every form of visual evidence.
explanation: The reading warns that popularity does not verify the story and asks readers to separate visible information from inference.

title: The value of a local name
author: Maya Chen
dek: A local place name can preserve a story about landscape, work or memory even after the original reason for it has faded.
body: A road may still be called Mill Lane although no mill remains. A hill's name may refer to a tree, a meeting place or a language that fewer residents now speak.
Names do not provide a complete history. They can be changed, misunderstood or used to hide a difficult past. Their value lies in giving people a clue to investigate rather than a final answer.
Listening to local names makes a familiar map less flat. It reminds residents that places are layered with earlier lives.
question: How should local place names be used as historical evidence?
options: As complete and unquestionable histories.
As clues that can lead to further questions about a place.
As labels that have no connection with earlier life.
explanation: The reading calls names clues rather than complete histories and encourages investigation of the stories behind them.

title: A frog crossing
author: Tara Singh
dek: A short seasonal road closure can protect amphibians when it follows the animals' movement rather than treating every day as identical.
body: On wet spring nights, frogs leave a pond to reach breeding places. Volunteers record where they cross the road and help guide them while a nearby section is closed for a few hours.
The measure is temporary and local. It does not solve every danger facing the animals, but it prevents a predictable conflict between traffic and a seasonal movement.
The project shows how observation can improve environmental decisions. A small change becomes effective because it is timed to the behaviour being protected.
question: Why is the road closure timed and local?
options: Because the animals move in a predictable seasonal pattern.
Because roads are never used outside spring.
Because volunteers want to prevent all traffic permanently.
explanation: The reading says the closure follows the frogs' seasonal movement and is limited to the relevant place and hours.

title: The school library shelf
author: Bea Williams
dek: A school library shelf can invite a wider range of readers when its organisation reflects different interests and ways of seeing.
body: A shelf arranged only by difficulty may help a teacher plan lessons, but it can make reading feel like a test. A display that also includes humour, sport, migration, fantasy and local history gives students more than one reason to choose a book.
The librarian still provides guidance. Students can ask for a shorter text, a challenge or a story that feels familiar, and the recommendations can change as the collection grows.
Variety does not mean abandoning structure. It means using structure to open choices rather than to predict what every reader will enjoy.
question: What is the purpose of variety on the school library shelf?
options: To remove all guidance from reading choices.
To offer students different reasons and routes into reading.
To organise books only by the number of pages.
explanation: The reading supports varied themes and guidance so students can choose through different interests and needs.

title: The noticeboard with three languages
author: Amara Cole
dek: A multilingual noticeboard is useful when translation is paired with a clear invitation to ask questions.
body: A community centre posts information in three common languages. The words help more people understand the date and place of an event, but a translation can still miss a local expression or an important detail.
Staff add pictures, contact hours and a sentence saying that help is available. Visitors can point to a notice, ask for clarification and suggest a better way to explain something.
Inclusion is not achieved by translating once and closing the process. It grows when information can be checked with the people who use it.
question: What makes the multilingual noticeboard more effective?
options: Using translation without allowing any questions.
Combining translated text with visual help and opportunities for clarification.
Posting only the longest possible version of each notice.
explanation: The reading adds pictures, contact details and dialogue so users can clarify and improve the information.

title: A bicycle bell
author: Jon Bell
dek: A bicycle bell is a small shared signal that can make movement safer when it communicates without accusation.
body: On a crowded path, a bell can tell a pedestrian that a bicycle is approaching. The sound gives both people a moment to adjust without requiring a shout or a sudden manoeuvre.
The signal works only when it is used early and respectfully. A bell is a request for attention, not a claim that one traveller automatically has priority.
Good street design supports this small conversation with enough space, clear surfaces and places where people can slow down.
question: When does a bicycle bell work well?
options: When it is used early as a respectful request for attention.
When it is used to prove that cyclists always have priority.
When it replaces the need for space on a path.
explanation: The reading describes the bell as an early, respectful signal supported by space and the possibility of slowing down.

title: The experiment with two cups
author: Sofia Reed
dek: A simple comparison becomes more informative when it changes one important condition and keeps the others similar.
body: A student wants to know whether sunlight affects a seedling. One cup is placed near a bright window and another in a darker corner, while the student uses similar soil and water for both.

The comparison is not perfect. The cups may differ in temperature, and two seedlings cannot represent every plant. Still, recording the method helps the student see what the result can and cannot show.
The experiment teaches a habit of careful questions: change one factor where possible, observe regularly and avoid claiming more than the evidence supports.
question: What habit does the two-cup experiment teach?
options: Changing many conditions at once.
Comparing carefully while recognising the limits of the evidence.
Treating two seedlings as proof about every plant.
explanation: The reading recommends changing one factor where possible and limiting the conclusion to what the comparison supports.

title: A phrase that changes meaning
author: Nora Ellis
dek: The meaning of a familiar phrase can shift with tone, relationship and the situation in which it is spoken.
body: 'That was clever' may be sincere praise after a difficult solution or a dry comment after an avoidable mistake. The words alone do not settle the speaker's intention.
Listeners use pauses, facial expression and shared history to interpret the phrase. A learner of the language may understand the dictionary meaning and still miss the social signal.
Learning expressions therefore involves more than memorising definitions. It requires noticing how people use them with one another.
question: Why can the phrase 'That was clever' be difficult to interpret?
options: Its dictionary meaning changes every day.
Its tone and context can make it praise or criticism.
It can be used only by people who know the same language.
explanation: The reading gives two opposite interpretations and attributes the difference to tone, relationship and context.

title: The manager who writes things down
author: Marcus Green
dek: Writing down a decision can help a team remember not only what it chose, but why it chose it.
body: After a meeting, people often remember the same discussion differently. A short note can record the decision, the person responsible and the reason that seemed most important at the time.

The note is not a contract against future learning. If new information appears, the team can change the decision and record why. The written record makes the change visible instead of making everyone reconstruct the past.
Documentation is useful when it serves memory and coordination rather than becoming a performance of detail.
question: What should a useful decision note record?
options: Every word spoken in the meeting.
The decision, responsibility and reasoning behind it.
Only the name of the manager.
explanation: The reading identifies the decision, the responsible person and the main reason as useful information.

title: The story of a repaired coat
author: Mara Okafor
dek: A repaired coat can carry the history of the people who kept it useful.
body: A tailor replaces a broken zip and patches the elbow of an old coat. The marks are visible, but the coat is warm and comfortable again.
The owner remembers wearing it on a first day at work and lending it to a brother during a cold winter. Repair has added another part to its story.
The coat is valuable not because it looks new, but because care has allowed it to continue being used.
question: Why is the repaired coat valuable to its owner?
options: It carries memories and can still be used.
It is the newest coat in the shop.
It has never needed any care.
explanation: The reading connects the coat's value with its memories, continued use and the care invested in it.

title: A seedling on a windowsill
author: Tara Singh
dek: A seedling teaches patience because its progress is easier to notice over several days than in one moment.
body: A child places a seed in a small pot and gives it a little water. At first, nothing seems to happen.
After a few days, a green shoot appears. The child measures it each morning and learns that growth can be slow and uneven.
Looking after the plant means observing it and changing the care when the soil is dry. The seedling rewards attention, not impatience.
question: What does the seedling teach the child?
options: Plants grow fully in one morning.
Growth can be slow and requires regular attention.
Plants never need their care to change.
explanation: The reading says the child observes slow growth and adjusts care when necessary.

title: The bus driver's map
author: Lina Haddad
dek: A bus driver uses a map to keep a journey clear when the usual route changes.
body: Roadworks close one street, so the driver checks a printed map before leaving the depot. The new route is longer, but it still connects the main stops.
The driver also tells passengers where to get off and shows them the temporary stop. Clear information helps people feel less worried about the change.
A map is useful because it supports both movement and communication.
question: What does the driver do besides choosing a new route?
options: Closes every stop on the bus line.
Asks passengers to find all the information themselves.
Explains the temporary stop and where passengers should get off.
explanation: The second paragraph says the driver gives passengers information about the temporary stop and exit point.

title: A book borrowed twice
author: Bea Williams
dek: Borrowing the same book twice can mean that a reader has changed, not that the first reading failed.
body: A reader borrows a novel at twelve and returns it after a few pages. Three years later, the reader chooses it again and understands much more of the story.
The words have not changed, but the reader's experience has. New knowledge and questions make different details stand out.
Returning to a book can therefore be a new kind of reading rather than a simple repetition.
question: Why can reading the book twice feel different?
options: The reader has changed and notices new details.
The library changes every word in the novel.
A book can be understood only after one page.
explanation: The reading says the reader's new knowledge and questions change what stands out in the second reading.

title: The message that can wait
author: Daniel Cho
dek: Not every message needs an immediate answer, and deciding when to reply can protect attention.
body: A phone shows a new message while someone is cooking, studying or talking to a friend. The message may be important, but it may not be urgent.
The person checks the subject, decides when to respond and returns to the activity. A short status message can also tell the sender when an answer is likely.
This small pause helps people use communication deliberately instead of letting every alert control the next minute.
question: What is the benefit of deciding when to reply?
options: Every message becomes unimportant.
A person can protect attention while still planning a response.
The phone stops receiving messages forever.
explanation: The reading describes a planned reply as a way to protect the current activity without ignoring communication.

title: The after-school snack
author: Grace Ito
dek: A simple after-school snack can help a child feel ready for homework or play.
body: A child comes home hungry and chooses yoghurt, fruit and a glass of water. The snack is quick to prepare and does not replace the evening meal.
Keeping a few easy foods in the kitchen means the child has a choice when the family is busy. An adult checks that food is stored safely and that the child can reach it.
The best snack is practical, enjoyable and suited to the rest of the day.
question: What makes the snack practical?
options: It takes several hours to prepare.
It replaces every other meal.
It is easy to prepare and fits between school and the evening meal.
explanation: The reading describes a quick snack that supports the afternoon without replacing the evening meal.

title: The clean-up that begins with sorting
author: Priya Shah
dek: A clean-up becomes more useful when people sort what they collect instead of putting everything in one bag.
body: Volunteers find bottles, paper, leaves and a broken chair in the park. They place different materials in separate containers.
Sorting shows which items can be recycled, composted or sent to a disposal centre. It also reveals what kind of rubbish appears most often.
The group leaves the park cleaner and learns how future events could prevent some waste.
question: What does sorting help the volunteers do?
options: Hide the rubbish from everyone.
Understand how materials should be handled and what waste is common.
Put every item into the same container.
explanation: The second paragraph says sorting identifies suitable handling and patterns in the rubbish collected.

title: The friend who explains a route
author: Amara Cole
dek: A clear explanation can make a new journey feel manageable.
body: A friend describes the walk to a new café: turn after the pharmacy, cross at the lights and look for a blue door.
The friend also sends a map and says which bus stop is closest. The traveller can choose the instructions that are easiest to remember.
Good help gives information without making the other person feel foolish for asking.
question: Why is the route explanation helpful?
options: It gives landmarks, options and a respectful answer to a question.
It says that maps are never useful.
It makes the traveller walk without crossing any streets.
explanation: The reading lists landmarks, a map and a bus-stop option, and emphasises respectful help.

title: The notebook on the train
author: Yuki Sato
dek: Writing down a new word when it appears can help a learner remember the situation in which it was used.
body: A passenger hears an unfamiliar word on the train and writes it in a notebook. Next to it, the passenger records the sentence and the place where it was heard.
Later, the learner checks the meaning and writes a new example. The word is easier to remember because it is connected to a real moment.
Good vocabulary practice includes both a definition and a context.
question: Why does the learner record the sentence as well as the word?
options: The sentence makes the notebook heavier.
The context creates another connection that can help memory.
A definition is never useful.
explanation: The reading says the word is easier to remember when it is connected to the real sentence and situation.

title: The club with a simple rule
author: June Okoro
dek: A club can work smoothly when one simple rule makes it easier for everyone to take part.
body: At the community photography club, members bring one picture and say one thing they noticed while taking it. Nobody has to use expensive equipment.
The rule gives each person a turn and keeps the discussion focused. Members learn from different ways of seeing the same street or object.
A small structure makes the group feel open rather than competitive.
question: What does the club's rule achieve?
options: It requires expensive cameras.
It makes every photograph identical.
It gives everyone a turn and keeps the discussion focused.
explanation: The second paragraph directly states that the rule gives each person a turn and keeps discussion focused.

title: The old photograph album
author: Clara Voss
dek: An old photograph album becomes more meaningful when people share the stories behind the pictures.
body: The album contains photographs of a family picnic, a wedding and a small seaside town. Some faces are easy to recognise, while others are not.
An older relative names the people and explains why one picture was taken. The family adds notes beside the photographs so that the information will not be lost.
The album keeps images, but conversation helps turn them into family history.
question: What does conversation add to the album?
options: It identifies people and explains the events in the pictures.
It removes the need to keep any photographs.
It makes every picture a professional photograph.
explanation: The relative's stories identify people and events, giving the images historical meaning.

title: The quiet corner
author: Lina Haddad
dek: A quiet corner in a busy building gives people a choice about how they spend time in public.
body: The library has tables for group work, but it also has a small corner with soft light and fewer conversations. A visitor sits there to read and recover from the noise outside.
The corner is not reserved for one kind of person. Anyone can use it, as long as they keep the space calm.
Different zones help a public building serve different needs at the same time.
question: Why is the quiet corner useful?
options: It prevents anyone from talking in the whole library.
It gives people a calm place while other activities continue elsewhere.
It is available only to library staff.
explanation: The reading contrasts the quiet zone with group tables and explains that both can operate in the same building.

title: The science fair question
author: Sofia Reed
dek: A good science fair project begins with a question that can be observed and tested.
body: A student wonders whether different amounts of water affect how quickly beans sprout. The student places similar seeds in three cups and records the water used each day.
The project cannot explain every plant, but it can compare the cups under the chosen conditions. The student writes down the method so another person can understand it.
A clear question turns curiosity into an investigation.
question: What makes the student's question suitable for an investigation?
options: It can be tested by comparing cups under recorded conditions.
It promises to explain every plant in the world.
It avoids measuring anything.
explanation: The reading says the question can be investigated through a recorded comparison of similar seeds and different water amounts.

title: The shared umbrella
author: Mara Okafor
dek: Sharing an umbrella is a small act of help that makes a rainy walk easier for two people.
body: A sudden shower begins outside the station. One person opens a large umbrella and invites a stranger walking in the same direction to stand underneath.
They move more slowly so that both can stay dry. At the corner, they thank each other and continue on separate routes.
The moment is brief, but it shows how a public place can contain ordinary acts of kindness.
question: What does the shared umbrella illustrate?
options: A brief act of kindness between strangers.
A rule that everyone must walk slowly in rain.
A way to avoid ever travelling alone.
explanation: The reading presents the umbrella as a short, voluntary act of help between two strangers.

title: The word on the window
author: Nadia Bell
dek: A word written on a shop window can welcome people by making information visible before they enter.
body: The bakery writes 'fresh bread' in large letters on the window. Another sign lists the day's opening hours and a greeting in two languages.
People passing by can understand what the shop offers and when it is open. Some stop because the greeting makes the place feel familiar.
Simple language can make a space easier to approach.
question: How does the writing on the window help?
options: It hides the bakery's opening hours.
It tells passers-by what is offered and makes the shop feel more approachable.
It prevents people from seeing the shop.
explanation: The signs provide information and a welcoming greeting, helping passers-by decide whether to enter.

title: The small repair box
author: Felix Moore
dek: Keeping a few basic tools nearby can turn a small household problem into a quick repair.
body: A box contains thread, needles, tape, glue and a small screwdriver. When a button comes loose, the tools are easy to find.
The repair takes only a few minutes. The person checks whether the item is safe to use and asks for help when the problem is more serious.
A repair box does not solve every problem, but it makes simple care more likely.
question: Why is the repair box useful?
options: It replaces every skilled repair service.
It makes damaged items impossible.
It keeps basic tools ready for small, safe repairs.
explanation: The reading says the box makes simple repairs convenient while recognising that serious problems may need help.

title: The bird at the feeder
author: Owen Price
dek: Watching a bird feeder can encourage careful observation when people record what they see instead of guessing.
body: A child sees a small bird at the feeder and notices its colours, size and behaviour. The child writes down the date and checks a guide later.
The bird may not be identified immediately. Looking again and comparing details is part of the activity.
Observation becomes more useful when curiosity is joined with patience.
question: What makes the child's observation useful?
options: The child records details and checks them carefully.
The child names the bird without looking closely.
The feeder guarantees that the same bird appears every day.
explanation: The reading highlights recording details, checking a guide and looking again rather than guessing.

title: The message board at work
author: Marcus Green
dek: A clear workplace message board helps a team notice small changes without interrupting every colleague.
body: The board shows today's room changes, deliveries and tasks that need attention. Each note includes a date and the name of the person who added it.
Workers check the board when they begin a shift. If a message is urgent or private, they speak directly to the right person instead.
The board supports routine information while leaving sensitive conversations elsewhere.
question: What kind of information belongs on the message board?
options: Private conversations about individual workers.
Routine updates such as room changes and deliveries.
Messages with no date or source.
explanation: The reading lists routine updates for the board and says urgent or private matters should be handled directly.

title: The museum ticket
author: Clara Voss
dek: A museum ticket can become a small memory of a visit when it records more than a price.
body: The ticket shows the exhibition title and the date. A visitor keeps it in a notebook and writes one sentence about a painting that was memorable.
Months later, the ticket brings back the visit and the question raised by the painting.
An ordinary object can hold a personal link to a cultural experience.
question: Why does the visitor keep the ticket?
options: It can be used to enter the museum every day.
It is the most expensive object in the exhibition.
Its details and note help bring back the memory of the visit.
explanation: The second and third paragraphs explain that the ticket and note preserve a memory of the exhibition.

title: The safer crossing
author: Jon Bell
dek: A crossing is easier to use when people can see one another and have enough time to move across.
body: The new crossing has bright markings, a lower kerb and a signal that gives pedestrians time to finish. Cars slow down because the road surface changes near the crossing.
The design helps children, older people and anyone carrying bags. It also makes the driver's next action clearer.
Small changes can make a street safer without making it impossible to travel through.
question: Which feature helps pedestrians finish crossing?
options: A signal that gives them enough time.
A higher kerb and faster traffic.
Road markings that cannot be seen.
explanation: The first paragraph specifically says the signal gives pedestrians time to finish crossing.

title: The water bottle
author: Priya Shah
dek: A reusable water bottle is most useful when a person remembers to carry and clean it.
body: A student fills a bottle before leaving home and takes it to class. The bottle can be used again instead of buying a new drink each day.
At the end of the day, the student washes it and leaves it open to dry. A reusable item still needs ordinary care.
The habit works because the bottle is easy to carry and ready for the next morning.
question: What helps the reusable bottle become a lasting habit?
options: Using it once and throwing it away.
Carrying it, cleaning it and preparing it for the next day.
Leaving it closed and unwashed.
explanation: The reading links the lasting habit with carrying, washing and preparing the bottle for reuse.

title: The class presentation
author: June Okoro
dek: A class presentation improves when the speaker explains one clear idea and gives the audience a way to follow it.
body: A student prepares three slides about a local river. The first names the subject, the second shows a photograph and the third explains one change the student noticed.
The student practises speaking slowly and looks up from the notes. The presentation is short, but the main idea is easy to remember.
Good communication is not about adding as much information as possible. It is about helping listeners understand what matters most.
question: What makes the presentation easy to follow?
options: It contains every fact about the river.
The speaker reads without looking up.
It presents one clear idea in a simple sequence.
explanation: The reading describes a short three-part sequence with one main idea and says this makes it memorable.

title: The neighbourhood notice
author: Amara Cole
dek: A good neighbourhood notice gives people enough information to decide whether an event is for them.
body: A notice on the community centre door announces a book exchange. It includes the date, opening hours, location and a sentence explaining what people can bring.
A phone number is included for questions. The notice is large enough to read from the pavement.
Clear information makes participation easier before anyone has to ask for help.
question: What information does the notice provide?
options: The date, place, times and what people can bring.
A list of books that nobody may exchange.
Only the name of the centre.
explanation: The first paragraph lists the date, hours, location and permitted items, while the second adds a contact number.

title: The bedtime book
author: Grace Ito
dek: A short bedtime book can help a family end the day with a calm shared activity.
body: A parent and child read two pages before turning off the light. The child asks about a character, and they talk briefly about the story.
The book does not need to be long or difficult. Choosing a regular time makes reading part of the evening routine.
The value lies in the shared attention as much as in finishing the story.
question: What is an important value of the bedtime book?
options: It must always be a long and difficult book.
It creates a calm moment of shared attention.
It prevents the child from asking questions.
explanation: The reading says the shared, calm attention matters as much as the length or completion of the story.

title: The lost button
author: Felix Moore
dek: Finding a lost button can reveal that a small object may be worth keeping for a practical reason.
body: A button falls from a favourite shirt. Instead of buying a new shirt, the owner looks in a small tin of spare buttons.
One button matches the colour, although it is not exactly the same shape. After it is sewn on, the shirt is ready to wear again.
A collection of small useful objects can prevent a simple problem from becoming a replacement.
question: What prevents the owner from replacing the shirt?
options: The shirt has no buttons.
The owner throws the shirt away immediately.
A spare button can be used to repair it.
explanation: The owner finds a matching spare button and repairs the shirt, so replacement is unnecessary.

title: The moon in a puddle
author: Owen Price
dek: A puddle can reflect the Moon, but the reflection is not the Moon itself.
body: After rain, a child sees a bright circle in a puddle. When a breeze moves the water, the circle shakes and breaks apart.
The Moon has not changed shape. The water is reflecting light towards the child's eyes, and the moving surface changes the reflection.
The puddle becomes a simple way to notice how light and surfaces work together.
question: Why does the circle in the puddle move?
options: The moving water changes the reflection.
The Moon falls into the puddle.
The sky becomes smaller after rain.
explanation: The reading explains that the moving water changes the reflected image while the Moon remains unchanged.

title: The clear label
author: Daniel Cho
dek: A clear label helps people understand whether an online image is news, an advertisement or a personal opinion.
body: A website places the words 'paid partnership' above a product photograph. Readers can then see that the image is part of a commercial message.
The label does not tell readers what to think about the product. It gives them information about the purpose of the post.
Honest labels make communication easier to judge.
question: What does the label tell readers?
options: That the product is perfect.
That the image is part of a paid commercial message.
That opinions are not allowed on the website.
explanation: The reading says the label identifies the post as a paid partnership without deciding what readers should think.

title: The market bag
author: Maya Chen
dek: A strong market bag is useful when it is carried often enough to replace many single-use bags.
body: A shopper keeps a folded cloth bag near the front door. It holds vegetables and bread at the Saturday market.
The shopper washes it when necessary and puts it back by the door. Forgetting it sometimes means using another bag, but the habit still reduces the number used over time.
A reusable item works best when it fits naturally into a person's routine.
question: What makes the market bag useful over time?
options: It must be used only once.
It is kept out of sight after shopping.
It is carried regularly, cared for and used instead of many disposable bags.
explanation: The reading links the bag's benefit with repeated use, care and fitting it into a routine.

title: The place for questions
author: June Okoro
dek: A help desk is more useful when people know that questions are expected rather than embarrassing.
body: At the library help desk, a sign says 'Questions welcome'. Staff explain how to find a book, use a computer or join a class.
Some visitors ask very simple questions, while others need more time. The staff answer both patiently.
The desk makes the library easier to use because people do not have to know everything before they arrive.
question: Why does the help desk make the library easier to use?
options: People can ask questions and receive patient guidance.
Visitors must know how everything works first.
The desk removes all books from the library.
explanation: The reading says the desk welcomes questions and gives patient guidance on several library services.

title: The new word notebook
author: Yuki Sato
dek: A vocabulary notebook is more useful when a learner adds examples instead of collecting isolated words.
body: A learner writes 'borrow' and 'lend' on the same page. Under each word, the learner adds a short sentence.
The examples show the difference between taking something for a time and giving something for a time. The learner reviews the page later in the week.
Examples and review help the words stay connected to meaning.
question: Why does the learner write examples?
options: To make the words impossible to compare.
To show how the words are used and how their meanings differ.
To avoid reviewing the vocabulary.
explanation: The examples make the difference between 'borrow' and 'lend' visible and connect each word with its use.

title: The friendly timetable
author: Marcus Green
dek: A timetable feels friendlier when it shows what must happen and leaves a little space for ordinary delays.
body: A volunteer team plans a morning event with times for setting up, welcoming guests and cleaning. They do not fill every minute with a task.
When a delivery arrives late, the team can adjust without losing the whole plan. A short break also helps people stay focused.
A useful timetable gives direction without pretending that real life is perfectly punctual.
question: Why does the timetable leave some space?
options: To avoid planning the event.
To make the event last forever.
To allow for delays and short breaks.
explanation: The second paragraph shows that spare time lets the team handle a late delivery and take a break.

title: The park map
author: Lina Haddad
dek: A park map helps visitors choose a route when it shows paths, facilities and places to rest.
body: The map marks the playground, toilets, pond and accessible path. A visitor can choose a short walk or a longer route around the trees.
Signs at the entrances match the colours on the map, so people can understand where they are.
Good information gives visitors more freedom to explore.
question: What does the park map help visitors do?
options: Choose a route that fits their needs.
Remove every tree from the park.
Find places that are not marked anywhere.
explanation: The marked paths and facilities let visitors choose between routes and find useful places.

title: The borrowed film
author: Mina Laurent
dek: Borrowing a film lets a family share a story without buying a copy they may watch only once.
body: A family chooses a film from the library's collection and watches it together on Friday evening. Afterwards, each person remembers a different favourite scene.
The film is returned on Monday so another family can use it. The collection allows many people to try different stories.
Sharing access can make culture part of ordinary routines.
question: Why is borrowing the film useful?
options: The family can keep it forever.
It lets the family share a story and then makes it available to others.
It means everyone must like the same scene.
explanation: The reading says borrowing supports a shared experience and allows another family to use the film afterwards.

title: The rain on the window
author: Tara Singh
dek: Watching rain on a window can help a child notice patterns in weather without needing a complicated instrument.
body: Drops run down the glass in different paths. Some join together, while others stop when they reach a dry spot.
The child watches the garden change colour and listens to the roof. Later, the child compares the wet morning with a dry afternoon.
Simple observation can make ordinary weather interesting.
question: What does the child do to learn from the rain?
options: Observes the drops and compares the wet and dry conditions.
Changes the weather with the window.
Stops looking as soon as the first drop falls.
explanation: The child observes the drops, the garden and the roof, then compares the wet morning with a dry afternoon.

title: The small promise
author: Grace Ito
dek: A small promise is easier to keep when it is specific enough to become a visible action.
body: A student promises to study more, but the plan feels vague. The student changes it to 'read ten pages after dinner on Monday and Wednesday'.
The action is limited and easy to check. If one evening is missed, the student can adjust the next session instead of abandoning the whole plan.
Clear promises turn good intentions into habits that can be observed and improved.
question: What makes the student's new promise more useful?
options: It names a specific action and time.
It promises to study every minute of the day.
It cannot be checked or adjusted.
explanation: The revised promise specifies what to do and when, making it visible and adaptable.

title: The neighbourhood sensor
author: Maya Chen
dek: The neighbourhood sensor shows why the neighbourhood sensor cannot be understood through one simple measure.
body: The neighbourhood sensor began with a practical question. Small systems often reveal larger questions about access and responsibility. The people involved first described what they needed and what had failed before.

The first version of the idea looked promising, but its limits became visible in daily use. The group adjusted the process, recorded objections and made space for people who had previously been overlooked. This made the result slower than a quick solution, but more reliable.

The example suggests that people compare convenience with its wider consequences. The neighbourhood sensor is therefore less a finished answer than a way of making a shared problem visible and manageable.
question: What is the main point of the reading?
options: The example works best when practical benefits are considered alongside access, limits and responsibility.
A quick solution is always better than one that includes different users.
The people involved avoid changing the original plan.
explanation: The reading presents the example as a practical change that becomes more reliable when its wider effects and different users are considered.
